questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with primary myelofibrosis, in which his bone marrow is progressively replaced by fibrosis, is found on exam to have a markedly enlarged liver and spleen. A biopsy of the liver shows clusters of developing erythroid and myeloid precursors. Reactivation of blood cell production in which of the following organs best explains these findings?

explanation.summary: When the bone marrow fails, blood formation can resume in former fetal sites such as the liver and spleen (extramedullary hematopoiesis).
explanation.whyCorrect: The liver and spleen serve as hematopoietic organs during fetal life and are normally inactive after birth, but they can revert to producing blood cells when the marrow cannot, such as in bone marrow failure or fibrosis. This is termed extramedullary hematopoiesis and produces the hepatosplenomegaly seen here.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The kidney secretes erythropoietin but is not itself a site of blood cell production.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The thymus supports T-cell maturation, not erythroid and myeloid precursor production, and is not a reserve hematopoietic organ.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Lymph nodes are secondary lymphoid tissue and are not described as sites that revert to hematopoiesis in marrow failure.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The yolk sac is an embryonic hematopoietic site that ceases and does not persist into postnatal life.

questions.stem: During a study of fetal development, blood cell production is examined at the fourth month of gestation. Which of the following organs is the chief site of hematopoiesis at this stage?

explanation.summary: The fetal liver is the main hematopoietic organ from roughly the second to the seventh month of gestation.
explanation.whyCorrect: After the earliest embryonic hematopoiesis, stem cells infiltrate the liver, which functions as the hematopoietic system from the second to the seventh months of gestation. The fourth month therefore falls within the period of dominant hepatic blood formation.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The bone marrow only takes over as the definitive site beginning around the seventh month of gestation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The yolk sac is the earliest embryonic site but its contribution has ended well before the fourth month.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The thymus is a site of T-cell maturation, not the primary organ of overall blood cell production.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The kidney produces erythropoietin later in life but is not a hematopoietic organ.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman requires a bone marrow biopsy to evaluate a suspected hematologic disorder. Which of the following is the preferred site for obtaining the marrow sample in this adult patient?

explanation.summary: The posterior iliac crest is the preferred site for bone marrow sampling in adults.
explanation.whyCorrect: The preferred site for bone marrow sampling is the posterior iliac crest (the back of the hip bone), which safely allows both aspiration and biopsy.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The sternum is used only as a last resort in adults, and only aspiration (not biopsy) is permitted because of the risk of injuring underlying structures.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The tibia is the preferred site in infants and young children, not in adults.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The distal radius is not a described site for marrow sampling.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The femoral shaft is not the preferred sampling location described for adults.

questions.stem: A 12-year-old boy with fatigue and easy bruising has a peripheral blood smear showing numerous immature blast cells that are normally confined to the bone marrow. This finding is most concerning for which of the following conditions?

explanation.summary: Blasts are normally restricted to the marrow; their appearance in peripheral blood suggests leukemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Blasts are normally present in the bone marrow but not in the peripheral blood. Their presence in the peripheral blood is concerning for leukemia, a condition in which blasts overgrow due to uncontrolled proliferation of neoplastic blast cells.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Iron deficiency anemia produces small, pale red cells, not circulating blasts.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Extramedullary hematopoiesis reflects blood formation resuming in organs such as the liver and spleen, not neoplastic blasts flooding the blood.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Reticulocytosis is an increase in immature red cells, not nucleated blast cells, and reflects red cell turnover rather than malignancy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: High-altitude polycythemia raises mature red cell mass through erythropoietin, without producing circulating blasts.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and chronic hypoxemia is found to have an elevated red blood cell count. The increased red cell production is driven by a hormone secreted mainly by cells in which of the following organs?

explanation.summary: Erythropoietin, the primary stimulator of red cell production, is secreted mainly by the kidney in response to hypoxia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Low systemic oxygen, as in chronic lung disease, causes the kidney to secrete erythropoietin, which can rise up to 1,000-fold during hypoxia. The kidney is the major source of erythropoietin, produced by renal interstitial cells around the peritubular capillaries of the renal cortex.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The liver is the primary erythropoietin source in the fetus and can make some in adults, but it is far less important than the kidney postnatally.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The spleen removes aged red cells but does not secrete erythropoietin.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The bone marrow is the target that responds to erythropoietin by making red cells, not the source of the hormone.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The heart is not involved in secreting hormones that stimulate blood cell production.

questions.stem: Circulating erythropoietin binds its receptor on red blood cell progenitor cells and stimulates their maturation. Activation of which of the following intracellular signaling pathways mediates this effect?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Gq protein with IP3 and diacylglycerol

choices.label: D
choices.text: Intracellular steroid hormone receptor acting on DNA

explanation.summary: Erythropoietin signals through a JAK/STAT tyrosine kinase cascade to drive red cell maturation.
explanation.whyCorrect: When circulating erythropoietin binds erythropoietin receptors on red blood cell progenitor cells at the first stage of differentiation, binding stimulates red cell maturation by activating a JAK/STAT tyrosine kinase signaling cascade.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The Gs/cAMP pathway is used by many peptide hormones but is not the mechanism described for erythropoietin.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The Gq/IP3/DAG pathway is a G-protein-coupled second messenger system, not the JAK/STAT cascade used by erythropoietin.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Erythropoietin is a peptide hormone acting at a cell-surface receptor, not a steroid acting on an intracellular DNA-binding receptor.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Guanylate cyclase/cGMP signaling is used by agents such as nitric oxide, not by erythropoietin.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman with long-standing chronic kidney disease has a normocytic anemia with an inappropriately low reticulocyte count. Which of the following best explains the underlying cause of her anemia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reduced erythropoietin production by damaged renal cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: Antibody-mediated destruction of circulating red cells

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dietary iron deficiency from poor intake

choices.label: D
choices.text: Vitamin B12 deficiency impairing DNA synthesis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Overproduction of erythropoietin causing marrow exhaustion

explanation.summary: Anemia of chronic kidney disease results largely from failure of damaged renal cells to make enough erythropoietin.
explanation.whyCorrect: In chronic kidney disease, renal cells are damaged and fail to make sufficient erythropoietin, leading to reduced hematopoiesis and anemia; shortened red cell survival contributes as well. The low reticulocyte count reflects inadequate erythropoietic stimulation, and treatment uses erythropoiesis-stimulating agents such as epoetin.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Antibody-mediated hemolysis would raise, not lower, the reticulocyte count as the marrow compensates.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Iron deficiency is a separate cause of anemia and is not the mechanism attributed to kidney disease here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Vitamin B12 deficiency causes a macrocytic anemia and is not the described renal mechanism.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Chronic kidney disease causes a deficiency, not an excess, of erythropoietin.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man presents with anemia. Laboratory studies show a markedly elevated reticulocyte count. This finding most strongly suggests that his anemia is caused by which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased destruction of red blood cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: Failure of erythropoiesis from bone marrow disease

choices.label: C
choices.text: Iron deficiency limiting red cell production

explanation.summary: A high reticulocyte count in an anemic patient points to increased red cell destruction, such as hemolysis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Reticulocytes are counted during evaluation of anemia. Increased counts are seen when anemia is caused by increased red blood cell destruction, such as hemolysis, because the marrow responds by releasing more immature red cells.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Failure of erythropoiesis from bone marrow disease produces a reduced, not elevated, reticulocyte count.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Iron deficiency reflects failure of red cell production and is associated with a low reticulocyte response.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Erythropoietin deficiency reduces erythropoietic drive and lowers the reticulocyte count.
whyWrongByChoice.E: An aplastic marrow lacks precursors and cannot mount a reticulocyte response, so the count would be low.

questions.stem: A histology student reviews the maturation of red blood cell precursors in the bone marrow. Which of the following changes normally occurs as the red cell matures?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The cytoplasm changes from deep blue to red

choices.label: C
choices.text: The nucleus enlarges as the cell matures

choices.label: D
choices.text: The chromatin becomes progressively less condensed

choices.label: E
choices.text: The nucleus is retained in the mature red blood cell

explanation.summary: During erythropoiesis the cytoplasm shifts from deep blue to red and develops a pale center as hemoglobin accumulates.
explanation.whyCorrect: As the red cell matures, the cytoplasm turns from deep blue to red and then develops a pale center due to peripheral localization of hemoglobin. The cell shrinks, the nucleus shrinks and disappears, and the chromatin evolves from very fine to very condensed.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The cell shrinks rather than enlarges during maturation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The nucleus shrinks, becomes darker, and disappears rather than enlarging.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The chromatin pattern evolves from very fine to very condensed, the opposite of becoming less condensed.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Mature red blood cells lack a nucleus, which is why they cannot divide.

questions.stem: A healthy adult donates blood, and the physiology of red cell turnover is discussed. Approximately how long does a normal red blood cell circulate in an adult before it is removed?

explanation.summary: Adult red blood cells circulate for roughly 120 days before macrophages remove them.
explanation.whyCorrect: Red blood cells are removed from the circulation when they are around 120 days old in an adult (and 60 to 90 days old in an infant). Old red cells are cleared by macrophages in the spleen and liver.

whyWrongByChoice.B: About a week is the approximate lifespan of a platelet, not a red blood cell.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Thirty days is far shorter than the roughly 120-day adult red cell lifespan.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Sixty days is closer to the lower end of the infant range, not the adult lifespan.
whyWrongByChoice.E: One year greatly overestimates the roughly 120-day red cell lifespan.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman develops severe neutropenia after receiving chemotherapy. She is treated with filgrastim, a recombinant form of granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF). Increasing the number of which of the following cells is the intended effect of this therapy?

explanation.summary: G-CSF (filgrastim) stimulates production of granulocytes and is used for granulocytopenia after chemotherapy.
explanation.whyCorrect: Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor increases granulocytes and can be used therapeutically as filgrastim (Neupogen) for granulocytopenia due to chemotherapy or other causes, or after bone marrow transplantation.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Platelet production is stimulated by thrombopoietin, not by G-CSF.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Red blood cell production is driven by erythropoietin, not G-CSF.
whyWrongByChoice.D: B-cell growth is promoted by cytokines such as IL-4, not by G-CSF.
whyWrongByChoice.E: G-CSF specifically raises granulocytes; agents such as M-CSF or GM-CSF are the ones tied to monocyte increases.

questions.stem: A megakaryocyte in the bone marrow is a very large cell, 50 to 100 micrometers in diameter, with a single prominent enlarged nucleus. This large polyploid nucleus develops through which of the following processes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Endomitosis, in which chromosomes replicate without nuclear division

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ordinary mitosis that splits the cell into two identical cells

choices.label: D
choices.text: Binary fission of the nucleus into separate nuclei

explanation.summary: Megakaryocytes enlarge through endomitosis, replicating DNA without dividing the nucleus or cell.
explanation.whyCorrect: When megakaryocyte precursors receive growth signals from thrombopoietin, they undergo endomitosis, a unique type of mitosis in which the nuclear and cellular components divide but do not separate. There is chromosomal replication without nuclear division, unlike ordinary mitosis and meiosis where cells split into two.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Meiosis produces haploid gametes and does not describe megakaryocyte enlargement.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Ordinary mitosis splits a cell into two identical cells, whereas endomitosis specifically avoids separation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The nucleus becomes a single enlarged polyploid nucleus rather than splitting into separate nuclei.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Apoptosis is programmed cell death, not the mechanism by which the megakaryocyte grows and becomes polyploid.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old woman comes to the clinic with worsening shortness of breath and blue-gray discoloration of her lips and nail beds. She recently started taking a nitrate for angina. Examination shows tachycardia and cyanosis. Pulse oximetry reads 85%, but arterial blood gas analysis shows a normal PaO2, and the cyanosis does not improve with supplemental oxygen. A drawn blood sample appears chocolate brown. Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment?

explanation.summary: Nitrate-induced acquired methemoglobinemia is treated with methylene blue.
explanation.whyCorrect: Nitrates/nitrites are oxidants that convert ferrous heme iron (Fe2+) to ferric iron (Fe3+), forming methemoglobin that cannot bind oxygen and produces cyanosis with chocolate-brown blood, a normal PaO2, and cyanosis refractory to oxygen. Methylene blue is the first-line treatment for acquired methemoglobinemia because it activates the NADPH-methemoglobin reductase pathway to reduce metHb back to functional hemoglobin.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Transfusion does not address the oxidized hemoglobin already present and is not the primary therapy for acquired methemoglobinemia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hydroxocobalamin is the antidote for cyanide poisoning, not methemoglobinemia; the vignette describes nitrate exposure.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The cyanosis is refractory to oxygen because the defect is oxidized hemoglobin, so hyperbaric oxygen would not correct it.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Iron corrects iron-deficiency anemia; the problem here is oxidation of existing heme iron, not iron deficiency.

questions.stem: A 7-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department with jaundice and dark urine two days after starting an antimalarial drug for travel prophylaxis. Laboratory studies show anemia, an elevated reticulocyte count, decreased haptoglobin, and increased serum LDH. A peripheral smear shows bite cells and red cells containing insoluble inclusions of denatured hemoglobin. A deficiency of which of the following enzymes best explains this presentation?

explanation.summary: Oxidant-triggered hemolysis with Heinz bodies and bite cells indicates G6PD deficiency.
explanation.whyCorrect: G6PD generates NADPH, the sole source of reducing power for glutathione-based antioxidant defense in the mature RBC. When an oxidative trigger (such as an antimalarial drug, infection, or fava beans) overwhelms this defense, hemoglobin is oxidatively denatured into insoluble Heinz bodies; splenic macrophages remove these, creating bite cells and hemolytic anemia.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Pyruvate kinase deficiency causes chronic hemolysis from ATP depletion, not oxidant-triggered episodes with Heinz bodies and bite cells.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Cytochrome b5 reductase deficiency causes methemoglobinemia (cyanosis, chocolate-brown blood), not oxidative hemolysis with bite cells.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Bisphosphoglycerate mutase makes 2,3-BPG to modulate hemoglobin affinity and is not linked to oxidant-triggered hemolysis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: LDH is elevated as a marker of hemolysis; its deficiency does not produce this oxidant-triggered picture.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old man is evaluated for an acute hemolytic episode that occurred during a febrile illness. A G6PD activity assay obtained during the acute event returns within the normal range, yet his clinical picture strongly suggests G6PD deficiency. Which of the following best explains the falsely normal enzyme activity?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Mature RBCs continuously synthesize new G6PD to replace defective enzyme

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reticulocytes contain higher, kinetically normal G6PD activity

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fever denatures the assay reagents, artificially raising measured activity

choices.label: D
choices.text: Catalase compensates by directly regenerating G6PD activity

choices.label: E
choices.text: Elevated 2,3-BPG interferes with the enzyme assay

explanation.summary: Reticulocytosis during hemolysis raises measured G6PD activity and can mask deficiency.
explanation.whyCorrect: Older RBCs have the lowest G6PD activity and lyse first, while newly released reticulocytes contain recently synthesized, kinetically normal G6PD. Testing during a hemolytic crisis, when the reticulocyte count is high, can yield falsely elevated activity. Guidelines therefore recommend testing about 3 months (roughly the 120-day RBC lifespan) after an episode.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Mature RBCs lack a nucleus and cannot synthesize new enzyme, which is precisely why aged cells accumulate defective G6PD.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The falsely normal result reflects the cell population sampled, not denatured assay reagents.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Catalase detoxifies hydrogen peroxide but does not regenerate or substitute for G6PD activity.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 2,3-BPG modulates hemoglobin oxygen affinity and does not interfere with the G6PD assay.

questions.stem: A 4-month-old infant has had jaundice and anemia since birth. Studies show chronic hemolysis, splenomegaly, and pigmented gallstones. Genetic testing identifies a deficiency of an erythrocyte enzyme that normally provides about half of the ATP produced by glycolysis. Which of the following best describes the mechanism by which this deficiency causes hemolysis?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Accumulation of oxidized hemoglobin forming Heinz bodies

choices.label: B
choices.text: Failure of ATP-dependent ion pumps, producing rigid, dehydrated red cells

choices.label: C
choices.text: Oxidation of heme iron from the ferrous to the ferric state

choices.label: D
choices.text: Impaired NADPH generation with loss of glutathione recycling

choices.label: E
choices.text: Increased hemoglobin oxygen affinity that traps oxygen in the RBC

explanation.summary: Pyruvate kinase deficiency depletes ATP, disabling ion pumps and making RBCs rigid and prone to lysis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Pyruvate kinase catalyzes the final ATP-generating step of glycolysis (about 50% of glycolytic ATP). Without adequate ATP, the Na+/K+ pump fails, cells lose water and shrink, intracellular hemoglobin concentration and cytosolic viscosity rise, and the membrane becomes rigid with erratic protrusions, making the cells vulnerable to shear forces and premature clearance.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Heinz bodies and oxidative denaturation characterize G6PD deficiency, not the ATP-depletion mechanism of pyruvate kinase deficiency.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Oxidation of heme iron describes methemoglobin formation, a different disorder.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Loss of NADPH and glutathione recycling is the mechanism of G6PD deficiency.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pyruvate kinase deficiency is actually associated with elevated 2,3-BPG that decreases affinity and improves oxygen delivery, not increased affinity.

questions.stem: A researcher studies how mature erythrocytes rapidly adjust the position of the oxygen dissociation curve. She focuses on a molecule synthesized in red cells specifically to lower hemoglobin affinity for oxygen. This molecule is produced from a glycolytic intermediate by bisphosphoglycerate mutase. Which of the following is this molecule?

explanation.summary: 2,3-BPG is made from 1,3-BPG by bisphosphoglycerate mutase to lower Hb oxygen affinity.
explanation.whyCorrect: In the Rapoport-Luebering shunt, bisphosphoglycerate mutase diverts the glycolytic intermediate 1,3-bisphosphoglycerate to form 2,3-BPG. 2,3-BPG stabilizes the deoxygenated state of hemoglobin, lowering oxygen affinity and shifting the curve to the right; RBCs modulate affinity rapidly by favoring its synthesis or degradation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Lactate is produced from pyruvate to regenerate NAD+ for glycolysis; it lowers pH but is not the product of bisphosphoglycerate mutase.
whyWrongByChoice.B: NADPH is generated by G6PD in the pentose phosphate pathway for antioxidant defense, not to modulate oxygen affinity.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Carbaminohemoglobin forms from CO2 binding hemoglobin, not from bisphosphoglycerate mutase.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Reduced glutathione is an antioxidant regenerated using NADPH, unrelated to bisphosphoglycerate mutase.

questions.stem: A physiology instructor explains why mature erythrocytes must produce lactate even when oxygen is abundant. Which of the following features of the mature red blood cell best accounts for this obligatory lactate production?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Absence of mitochondria, limiting ATP production to glycolysis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Presence of abundant fatty acid oxidation enzymes

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dependence on insulin for glucose uptake

choices.label: D
choices.text: A high rate of oxidative phosphorylation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Lack of the enzyme glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate dehydrogenase

explanation.summary: Lacking mitochondria, RBCs rely on glycolysis and regenerate NAD+ by reducing pyruvate to lactate.
explanation.whyCorrect: Mature erythrocytes lack all organelles, including mitochondria, so ATP production is limited to substrate-level phosphorylation in glycolysis. To keep glycolysis running, NAD+ must be regenerated, which is accomplished by reducing pyruvate to lactate; complete oxidation of glucose is impossible without mitochondria.

whyWrongByChoice.B: RBCs lack mitochondria and do not perform fatty acid (beta) oxidation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: RBCs take up glucose via GLUT1 independent of insulin and lack insulin receptors.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Without mitochondria, RBCs cannot perform oxidative phosphorylation at all.
whyWrongByChoice.E: GAPDH is present and functions in glycolysis; its NADH also supports metHb reduction via cytochrome b5 reductase.

questions.stem: During prolonged fasting, most tissues shift their fuel utilization, yet red blood cells continue to rely on the same fuel source regardless of dietary state. Consistent with this behavior, mature erythrocytes lack receptors for both insulin and glucagon. Which of the following best explains the unwavering reliance of RBCs on this single fuel?

choices.label: A
choices.text: They store glycogen for use during fasting

choices.label: B
choices.text: They oxidize ketone bodies when glucose is scarce

choices.label: C
choices.text: They depend on glycolysis and use GLUT1 to take up glucose

choices.label: D
choices.text: They perform gluconeogenesis to sustain their own glucose supply

choices.label: E
choices.text: They switch to fatty acids as their primary energy source

explanation.summary: RBCs use glucose exclusively via insulin-independent GLUT1 transport in all dietary states.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because RBCs depend entirely on glycolysis for ATP, they must take in circulating glucose continuously. They use GLUT1 to transport glucose even at low concentrations seen with prolonged fasting, and because their fuel does not change with dietary state, they have no need for insulin or glucagon receptors.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Mature RBCs do not store glycogen; they rely on circulating glucose.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Ketone body oxidation requires mitochondria, which RBCs lack.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Gluconeogenesis occurs in the liver, not in erythrocytes.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Fatty acid oxidation requires mitochondria and does not occur in RBCs.

questions.stem: In the mature red blood cell, the pentose phosphate pathway is the only source of a reducing cofactor essential for antioxidant defense. Loss of this cofactor impairs the regeneration of reduced glutathione. Which of the following cofactors is produced by glucose 6-phosphate dehydrogenase to serve this role?

explanation.summary: G6PD produces NADPH, the sole reducing power in RBCs for regenerating glutathione.
explanation.whyCorrect: G6PD catalyzes the first reaction of the pentose phosphate pathway and generates NADPH. In mature RBCs the PPP is the sole source of NADPH, which glutathione reductase uses to regenerate reduced glutathione (GSH) from its oxidized dimer; GSH then neutralizes oxidants via glutathione peroxidase.

whyWrongByChoice.A: NADH is produced by GAPDH in glycolysis and feeds cytochrome b5 reductase, not glutathione recycling.
whyWrongByChoice.B: FADH2 is generated in mitochondrial pathways, which RBCs lack.
whyWrongByChoice.C: ATP is the energy currency from glycolysis, not the reducing cofactor for glutathione.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Thiamine pyrophosphate is a coenzyme for transketolase, not the product of G6PD used to regenerate glutathione.

questions.stem: A newborn is noted to have persistent cyanosis and chocolate-brown blood, but arterial PaO2 is normal and the cyanosis does not respond to oxygen. There is no cardiac or pulmonary disease. The infant is found to have a congenital deficiency of the enzyme that normally keeps methemoglobin levels below about 1%. Which of the following enzymes is deficient?

explanation.summary: Cytochrome b5 reductase normally reduces metHb, and its deficiency causes congenital methemoglobinemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Methemoglobin is maintained at very low levels chiefly by cytochrome b5 reductase, which uses NADH formed by GAPDH in glycolysis to reduce ferric heme back to functional ferrous hemoglobin. A congenital deficiency of this enzyme is a cause of congenital methemoglobinemia, presenting with cyanosis and chocolate-brown blood with a normal PaO2.

whyWrongByChoice.A: G6PD deficiency causes oxidative hemolytic anemia; the NADPH-metHb reductase pathway contributes minimally to routine metHb reduction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pyruvate kinase deficiency causes chronic hemolytic anemia from ATP depletion, not methemoglobinemia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Glutathione peroxidase neutralizes oxidants but does not reduce methemoglobin.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Bisphosphoglycerate mutase produces 2,3-BPG and does not reduce methemoglobin.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man with known G6PD deficiency develops symptomatic acquired methemoglobinemia after a chemical exposure. The physician notes that the usual first-line treatment is relatively discouraged in this patient. Which of the following best explains why methylene blue is contraindicated in this setting?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Methylene blue oxidizes ferrous iron to ferric iron, worsening metHb

choices.label: B
choices.text: Reduction of methemoglobin by methylene blue is NADPH-dependent, and these patients have a diminished NADPH pool

choices.label: C
choices.text: Methylene blue inhibits cytochrome b5 reductase

choices.label: D
choices.text: Methylene blue depletes 2,3-BPG, raising hemoglobin oxygen affinity

choices.label: E
choices.text: Methylene blue is only effective for congenital forms of methemoglobinemia

explanation.summary: Methylene blue works through an NADPH-dependent pathway that G6PD-deficient patients cannot support.
explanation.whyCorrect: Methylene blue reduces methemoglobin by activating NADPH-methemoglobin reductase, which relies on NADPH from the pentose phosphate pathway. G6PD-deficient patients already have a diminished NADPH pool, so stimulating an additional NADPH-requiring pathway can potentiate hemolysis, making methylene blue relatively contraindicated.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Methylene blue reduces, not oxidizes, methemoglobin; it does not worsen metHb by oxidation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Methylene blue does not inhibit cytochrome b5 reductase; it acts through the separate NADPH-dependent enzyme.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The contraindication relates to NADPH dependence, not to 2,3-BPG depletion.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Methylene blue is the typical treatment for acquired forms and is actually less effective for congenital forms.

questions.stem: During vigorous exercise, working skeletal muscle becomes warm and acidic and accumulates carbon dioxide. These local conditions alter the oxygen dissociation curve of hemoglobin. Which of the following best describes the effect on hemoglobin and its physiologic benefit?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased oxygen affinity, promoting oxygen loading in tissues

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased oxygen affinity, promoting oxygen delivery to tissues

choices.label: C
choices.text: No change in affinity, since local conditions do not affect hemoglobin

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increased oxygen affinity, shifting the curve to the left

choices.label: E
choices.text: Decreased oxygen affinity, impairing oxygen release to tissues

explanation.summary: Low pH, high pCO2, and high temperature shift the curve right, unloading oxygen where it is needed.
explanation.whyCorrect: Low pH (the Bohr effect), elevated pCO2, and high temperature each decrease hemoglobin oxygen affinity and shift the dissociation curve to the right (higher p50). In active, warm, acidic, CO2-rich muscle, this decreased affinity promotes release of oxygen to the tissues that need it most.

whyWrongByChoice.A: These conditions decrease, not increase, affinity, and a right shift favors unloading rather than loading.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Local pH, CO2, and temperature clearly do alter hemoglobin affinity.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A left shift with increased affinity is the opposite of the Bohr effect produced by these conditions.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Decreased affinity actually enhances oxygen release to tissues, which is the physiologic benefit, not an impairment.

questions.stem: A patient with confirmed methemoglobinemia has a pulse oximetry reading of approximately 85% that does not change despite escalating supplemental oxygen. Which of the following is the most reliable method to accurately measure this patient's methemoglobin level?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Calculated oxygen saturation from PaO2 on arterial blood gas

explanation.summary: Co-oximetry is required to accurately quantify methemoglobin, which confounds pulse oximetry.
explanation.whyCorrect: Methemoglobin interferes with wavelength-based pulse oximetry, driving the reading toward roughly 85% regardless of true saturation. Because pulse oximetry is unreliable, co-oximetry, which measures multiple hemoglobin species directly, is needed to obtain an accurate methemoglobin level.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Pulse oximetry is exactly what is confounded by methemoglobin and reads about 85% regardless of severity.
whyWrongByChoice.B: PaO2 is typically normal in methemoglobinemia, so a calculated saturation overestimates oxygenation and does not measure metHb.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A blood smear evaluates cell morphology but does not quantify methemoglobin.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Haptoglobin is a marker of hemolysis and does not measure methemoglobin levels.

questions.stem: A 38-year-old woman presents with 3 months of fatigue and heavy menstrual bleeding. Her hemoglobin is 9.8 g/dL and serum ferritin is low, consistent with iron-deficiency anemia. Which additional red blood cell index finding is most likely on her complete blood count?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Red blood cell count above the reference range

choices.label: E
choices.text: Mean cell hemoglobin concentration of 40 g/dL

explanation.summary: Iron-deficiency anemia produces small (microcytic), hemoglobin-poor red cells, so the MCV falls below the normal 80-100 fL range.
explanation.whyCorrect: The MCV measures the average volume (size) of the RBCs, with a reference range of 80-100 fL. Iron deficiency limits hemoglobin synthesis, yielding smaller-than-normal (microcytic) red cells, so an MCV of 72 fL fits the picture.

whyWrongByChoice.B: An MCV of 105 fL is macrocytic and points to causes such as B12 or folate deficiency, not iron deficiency.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Anemia lowers the hematocrit; a hematocrit of 52% is at the high end of normal and would not be seen in an anemic patient.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Anemia is characterized by a decrease in RBC mass, so the RBC count is typically low, not above the reference range.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The MCHC reference range is 32-36 g/dL; iron-deficient cells are hypochromic (low MCHC), not 40 g/dL.

questions.stem: A hematology resident is reviewing a complete blood count and wants to know how much the patient's red blood cell sizes vary from one another. Which red blood cell index most directly reflects this variation in cell size?

explanation.summary: The RDW quantifies the variability of the MCV, and a wide range of red cell sizes is termed anisocytosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: The red blood cell distribution width (reference range 12-15%) describes the variability of the MCV. When some cells are small and others large, the RDW rises; this variation in RBC size is called anisocytosis and helps distinguish among types of anemia.

whyWrongByChoice.A: MCH is the average weight of hemoglobin per RBC, not a measure of size variation.
whyWrongByChoice.B: MCHC is the average concentration of hemoglobin per RBC and relates to color (chromicity), not size spread.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hematocrit is the percentage of blood volume made up of RBCs and says nothing about how uniform the cells are.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hemoglobin measures the grams of Hgb per volume of blood and does not indicate variation in cell size.

questions.stem: A medical student is learning why a Wright-Giemsa stain colors different parts of a blood cell differently. On this gold-standard stain, which cellular component takes up eosin and appears red?

explanation.summary: On Wright-Giemsa staining, positively charged hemoglobin binds eosin and stains red (eosinophilic), while negatively charged nucleic acids bind methylene blue (basophilic).
explanation.whyCorrect: The Wright-Giemsa stain contains eosin, which stains structures red, and methylene blue, which stains structures blue. Positively charged compounds such as hemoglobin are eosinophilic and stain red, whereas negatively charged compounds like DNA and RNA are basophilic and stain blue.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Nuclear DNA is negatively charged and basophilic, so it takes up methylene blue and stains blue, not red.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Ribosomal RNA is negatively charged and basophilic; it also stains blue rather than red.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Negatively charged nucleic acids bind methylene blue and appear blue (basophilic), the opposite of eosinophilic red.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Reticulocyte RNA is basophilic and, moreover, is best seen with supravital stains, not colored red by eosin.

questions.stem: A 63-year-old man with chronic myelofibrosis has a peripheral blood smear reviewed. Which red blood cell morphology is most characteristically associated with this marrow disorder?

explanation.summary: Teardrop cells (dacrocytes) are the classic red cell shape seen in myelofibrosis and other marrow-occupying processes.
explanation.whyCorrect: Dacrocytes, or teardrop cells, are associated with chronic myelofibrosis and other space-occupying lesions of the marrow, where red cells are deformed as they squeeze out of a fibrotic marrow. This is the expected finding on this patient's smear.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Target cells (codocytes) are seen in thalassemia, liver disease, and iron deficiency, not myelofibrosis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Bite cells are seen in glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Sickle cells are the hallmark of sickle cell anemia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Schistocytes are fragmented cells seen in microangiopathic hemolytic anemia.

questions.stem: A 7-year-old boy underwent splenectomy after traumatic splenic rupture. A follow-up blood smear shows red cells each containing a single round, dark-blue dot representing a residual nuclear DNA remnant. Which inclusion is being described?

explanation.summary: Howell-Jolly bodies are nuclear DNA remnants normally removed by the spleen, so they appear after splenectomy or in functional asplenia.
explanation.whyCorrect: A Howell-Jolly body is a residual fragment of nuclear DNA that a healthy spleen would ordinarily pit from the red cell. After splenectomy the spleen can no longer remove them, so they persist as a single round dark-blue inclusion, consistent with this post-splenectomy patient.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Basophilic stippling is diffuse fine blue granules of aggregated ribosomal RNA seen in lead poisoning and thalassemia, not a single DNA dot.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pappenheimer bodies are iron-containing granules seen in sideroblastic anemia and cluster at the cell periphery.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Plasmodium organisms are intracellular parasites (ring forms) diagnostic of malaria.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Rouleaux formation is stacking of red cells due to increased immunoglobulins, not an intracellular inclusion.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old child with anemia and abdominal pain is found to have lived in an old home with peeling paint. The blood smear shows red cells with diffuse fine blue granules distributed throughout the cytoplasm. Which inclusion, and its underlying material, best explains this finding?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Basophilic stippling composed of aggregated ribosomal RNA

choices.label: B
choices.text: Howell-Jolly body composed of nuclear DNA

choices.label: C
choices.text: Pappenheimer bodies composed of iron granules

choices.label: D
choices.text: Plasmodium ring forms composed of parasites

choices.label: E
choices.text: Target cells composed of excess membrane

explanation.summary: Diffuse fine blue granules of aggregated ribosomal RNA (basophilic stippling) are seen in lead poisoning and thalassemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Basophilic stippling appears as fine blue granules spread throughout the red cell and represents aggregated ribosomal RNA. The classic associations are lead poisoning and thalassemia, fitting this child's lead-exposure history.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A Howell-Jolly body is a single round dot of nuclear DNA seen after splenectomy, not diffuse granules.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pappenheimer bodies are iron-containing granules of sideroblastic anemia that cluster at the cell edge rather than distributing diffusely.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Plasmodium ring forms indicate malaria and are discrete parasites, not fine RNA granules.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Target cells are a shape abnormality, not an inclusion, and are unrelated to lead exposure.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman with chronic anemia is being evaluated, and her physician wants to visualize the ribosomal RNA inside her reticulocytes. Which staining method is most appropriate?

explanation.summary: Supravital stains such as new methylene blue and brilliant cresyl blue highlight the ribosomal RNA of reticulocytes, which is not well seen on routine smears.
explanation.whyCorrect: Supravital stains are applied to fresh, living cells and reveal structures not visible on dried Wright-Giemsa smears. New methylene blue and brilliant cresyl blue precipitate the residual ribosomal RNA of reticulocytes into a visible blue network, allowing the reticulocyte count.

whyWrongByChoice.A: On Wright-Giemsa staining reticulocyte RNA is only faintly detectable as a lilac tinge (polychromasia) and is not directly visualized.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Periodic acid-Schiff stains carbohydrates, not ribosomal RNA.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Sudan black B stains lipids and is used to characterize leukemias, not reticulocyte RNA.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The Coombs reagent detects antibody or complement coating red cells; it does not stain RNA.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with autoimmune hemolytic anemia has a hemoglobin of 8 g/dL and a hematocrit of 27%. Her measured reticulocyte count is 6%. Which interpretation of her bone marrow response is most accurate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The marrow is hypoproliferative and failing to respond

choices.label: B
choices.text: The marrow is appropriately responding to the anemia

choices.label: C
choices.text: The reticulocyte count is within the normal reference range

choices.label: D
choices.text: The elevated reticulocyte count indicates the cells are macrocytic artifacts

choices.label: E
choices.text: The finding indicates the red cells are coated with antibody

explanation.summary: A reticulocyte count above 2%, especially when corrected for the degree of anemia, indicates a hyperproliferative marrow responding to hemolysis or blood loss.
explanation.whyCorrect: Normally 1-2% of circulating RBCs are reticulocytes. A count above 2% signals a hyperproliferative (responding) marrow, typical of hemolysis or hemorrhage. In this hemolytic patient the elevated reticulocyte count shows the marrow is appropriately ramping up red cell production.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A hypoproliferative marrow is reflected by a reticulocyte count below 2%; a value of 6% is the opposite.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The normal reference range is about 1-2%, so 6% is clearly elevated, not normal.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A high reticulocyte count reflects true increased production, not a macrocytic artifact.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Antibody coating of red cells is detected by the direct antiglobulin test, not inferred from the reticulocyte count.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old woman is engaged to a man with sickle cell trait and wants to know whether she carries an abnormal hemoglobin before they have children. Her screening CBC is normal. Which test best identifies and quantifies the different types of hemoglobin in her blood?

explanation.summary: Hemoglobin electrophoresis separates hemoglobin types by charge and quantifies them, making it the screening test for hemoglobinopathies such as sickle cell trait.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hemoglobin electrophoresis applies an electric charge to a gel so different hemoglobins migrate to characteristic positions (order A, F, S, C from farthest to closest). It identifies and quantifies hemoglobins, so it can detect hemoglobin S and confirm or exclude sickle cell trait even when the CBC is normal.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Osmotic fragility testing measures how readily RBCs lyse in hypotonic saline and is used for hereditary spherocytosis, not for identifying hemoglobin types.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The direct antiglobulin test detects antibody or complement on red cells in immune hemolysis, not hemoglobin variants.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The reticulocyte count assesses marrow production and does not distinguish hemoglobin types.
whyWrongByChoice.E: RDW measures variation in red cell size and cannot identify an abnormal hemoglobin.

questions.stem: A 20-year-old man with lifelong mild anemia, jaundice, and splenomegaly has a smear showing round red cells that lack central pallor. On osmotic fragility testing his red cells lyse at higher-than-normal concentrations of sodium chloride. Which diagnosis best fits these findings?

choices.label: E
choices.text: Autoimmune hemolytic anemia from IgG coating

explanation.summary: Increased osmotic fragility, with lysis at higher-than-normal NaCl concentrations, is characteristic of hereditary spherocytosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Spherocytes in hereditary spherocytosis have cytoskeleton mutations that make them less deformable and more prone to rupture, so they lyse even in less hypotonic (higher NaCl) solutions, giving increased osmotic fragility. The round cells lacking central pallor further support the diagnosis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Sickle cell anemia is associated with decreased, not increased, osmotic fragility.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Thalassemia is also associated with decreased osmotic fragility because of thin, target-shaped cells.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Iron-deficiency anemia produces microcytic hypochromic cells and does not typically cause increased osmotic fragility or spherocytes lacking central pallor.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Autoimmune hemolytic anemia is diagnosed with a positive direct antiglobulin test; the scenario points to a membrane defect rather than antibody coating.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman develops worsening anemia. A direct antiglobulin (Coombs) test is performed: when Coombs reagent is added to her washed red cells, they agglutinate. What does this positive result indicate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Her red cells are coated with antibody or complement

choices.label: B
choices.text: Her red cells contain an abnormal hemoglobin

choices.label: C
choices.text: Her red cells are excessively fragile in hypotonic saline

choices.label: D
choices.text: Her marrow is producing too many reticulocytes

choices.label: E
choices.text: Her red cells are microcytic and hypochromic

explanation.summary: A positive direct antiglobulin test means the patient's red cells are already coated with immunoglobulin or complement, as in autoimmune hemolytic anemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: In the direct antiglobulin test, anti-immunoglobulin/anti-complement (Coombs) reagent is added to the patient's red cells. If the cells are coated with antibody or complement, the reagent cross-links them and they agglutinate, giving a positive result seen in autoimmune hemolytic anemia, transfusion reactions, hemolytic disease of the newborn, and drug-induced hemolysis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Abnormal hemoglobins are detected by hemoglobin electrophoresis, not by the antiglobulin test.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Fragility in hypotonic saline is assessed by osmotic fragility testing, a different assay.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Reticulocyte production is measured by the reticulocyte count, which the Coombs test does not evaluate.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Cell size and chromicity are read from the MCV and MCHC, not from agglutination with Coombs reagent.

questions.stem: An elderly patient's automated CBC returns a very low red blood cell count with a strikingly high MCV. The technologist notes clumping of red cells on the smear consistent with cold agglutinins. Which of the following values is most likely to remain accurate despite the agglutination?

explanation.summary: When red cells agglutinate, clumps are miscounted so the RBC count falls and MCV rises falsely, but hemoglobin stays accurate because it is measured after the cells are lysed.
explanation.whyCorrect: Automated analyzers classify particles by size, so agglutinated clumps are counted as fewer, larger cells: the RBC count is falsely decreased and the MCV falsely increased. Hemoglobin is measured spectrophotometrically after the red cells are lysed, so clumping does not affect it, and it usually remains accurate.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The RBC count is falsely decreased because clumps are undercounted as single particles.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The MCV is falsely increased because clumps are read as abnormally large single cells.
whyWrongByChoice.D: MCH is derived from hemoglobin and the falsely low RBC count, so it is falsely increased.
whyWrongByChoice.E: MCHC is likewise falsely increased when agglutination distorts the RBC-derived measurements.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman is evaluated for fatigue. Her physician explains that a clinical diagnosis of anemia is based on comparing a single laboratory value to an age- and sex-matched reference population. Which of the following laboratory parameters is used to establish this clinical diagnosis?

explanation.summary: The clinical definition of anemia is a decreased hemoglobin (or hematocrit) relative to a reference population.
explanation.whyCorrect: Anemia is defined clinically as a hemoglobin (or hematocrit) below the reference range for a matched population, which is derived from the mean +/- 2 standard deviations. Hemoglobin is therefore the value used to establish the diagnosis before further indices refine the differential.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The DAT detects antibodies on the red cell surface and is a test for autoimmune hemolysis, not a screen for anemia itself.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Indirect bilirubin is a marker of hemolysis (increased red cell turnover), not the parameter that defines anemia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: MCV measures red cell size and is used to narrow the differential only after anemia has already been established.
whyWrongByChoice.E: RDW measures variability in red cell size and helps narrow the differential once anemia is diagnosed, but it does not define anemia.

questions.stem: A 16-year-old boy is admitted to the emergency department with a 2-week history of diarrhea, sepsis, hypovolemic shock, and acute kidney injury. His admission hemoglobin is 15.3 g/dL. A repeat complete blood count less than 24 hours later, after large boluses of intravenous fluids, shows a hemoglobin of 9.9 g/dL, along with decreases in his white blood cell count and platelet count. Which of the following best explains the drop in hemoglobin?

explanation.summary: A hemoglobin that falls acutely after large IV fluid boluses, together with drops in WBC and platelet counts, indicates a dilutional (spurious) anemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hemoglobin is a ratio of grams of hemoglobin per deciliter of blood. Expanding the plasma volume (denominator) with IV fluids lowers the apparent hemoglobin without true red cell loss. A helpful clue that the drop is dilutional is that the white cell and platelet counts fall in parallel.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Intravascular hemolysis would not simultaneously lower the white cell and platelet counts, and there is no jaundice or dark urine described.
whyWrongByChoice.C: GI hemorrhage causes a true drop in red cell mass and would not proportionally lower the WBC and platelet counts.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Marrow suppression develops over days to weeks and cannot explain a hemoglobin change within 24 hours tied to fluid administration.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Iron deficiency is a chronic process and cannot account for an acute hemoglobin change over less than a day.

questions.stem: A 10-week-old, previously healthy, full-term infant is found to have a hemoglobin of 9.8 g/dL on routine testing. He is feeding and growing well and is otherwise asymptomatic. Which of the following best explains this finding?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Dilutional anemia from congestive heart failure

explanation.summary: Infants normally experience a physiologic anemia around 8-12 weeks of age with a nadir that can reach 9.5-10 g/dL.
explanation.whyCorrect: After birth, red cell mass declines in response to increased oxygen availability and downregulation of erythropoietin. The count falls until oxygen delivery becomes inadequate and erythropoietin production resumes, producing an expected physiologic nadir at roughly 8-12 weeks. An age-matched reference range must be used because normal hemoglobin varies with age.

whyWrongByChoice.B: GI blood loss is not suggested in an asymptomatic, well-growing infant and would be an uncommon explanation at this age.
whyWrongByChoice.C: An inherited hemolytic anemia would typically produce jaundice, dark urine, or a positive family history, none of which are described.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Dilutional anemia from CHF requires plasma-volume expansion from heart failure, which this healthy infant does not have.
whyWrongByChoice.E: No medication exposure is described, and folate antagonists cause a maturation-defect anemia, not the expected physiologic nadir.

questions.stem: A 27-year-old woman at 26 weeks gestation is noted to have a hemoglobin of 10.6 g/dL. She is asymptomatic, and her white cell and platelet counts are normal. Which of the following physiologic changes most likely accounts for the mildly decreased hemoglobin?

explanation.summary: Pregnancy causes a physiologic (dilutional) anemia because plasma volume increases relative to red cell mass.
explanation.whyCorrect: In normal pregnancy the plasma volume expands more than the red cell mass, modestly lowering the hemoglobin concentration. Anemia in pregnancy is typically defined as less than 11 g/dL in the first and third trimesters and less than 10.5 g/dL in the second trimester, so this value is near the physiologic range.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Chronic GI blood loss is not suggested here and would cause a true iron deficiency rather than a physiologic dilutional change.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Testosterone raises hemoglobin (an erythropoietic effect) and would not explain a decrease.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Autoimmune hemolysis would be accompanied by jaundice or dark urine and is not indicated by an isolated mild hemoglobin drop.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Marrow aplasia typically depresses all cell lines, but this patient's white cell and platelet counts are normal.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with fatigue and pallor. She reports heavy periods lasting 7 days, using 5 pads per day with occasional clots, and she has four children ranging from 2 to 6 years of age. She was told she was anemic during a previous pregnancy. She eats a regular diet with meat, has no change in bowel habits or dark stools, and has had no jaundice or dark urine. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her anemia?

explanation.summary: Heavy menstrual loss combined with several closely spaced pregnancies is a classic history for iron deficiency anemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Menstrual blood loss is a very common cause of iron deficiency anemia, and each pregnancy costs roughly 600-750 mg of iron. Several pregnancies at short intervals give the body little time to replenish lost iron, and the absence of hemolysis, GI bleeding, or dilutional features points to iron deficiency from extensive menstrual and pregnancy-related loss.

whyWrongByChoice.B: There is no jaundice, dark urine, or other evidence of hemolysis to support an autoimmune hemolytic process.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Aplastic anemia would typically depress all cell lines and is not suggested by this focused blood-loss history.
whyWrongByChoice.D: No chronic infection, inflammatory disease, or malignancy is described to invoke anemia of chronic inflammation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: She is not pregnant now and has no CHF, splenomegaly, or recent IV fluids to cause plasma-volume expansion.

questions.stem: During a history for anemia, a physician emphasizes that each full-term pregnancy represents a large obligate iron loss that predisposes to iron deficiency, particularly with multiple closely spaced pregnancies. Approximately how much iron is lost per pregnancy?

explanation.summary: Each pregnancy represents roughly 600-750 mg of iron loss.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick states that each pregnancy represents about 600-750 mg of iron lost, a large amount to replace. Women who have had multiple pregnancies, especially at short intervals, are highly likely to become iron deficient.

whyWrongByChoice.A: 20-30 mg is far too small; it is closer to daily dietary iron content, not the cost of a pregnancy.
whyWrongByChoice.B: 100-150 mg substantially underestimates the obligate iron loss of a pregnancy.
whyWrongByChoice.C: About 200 mg is the iron loss associated with one unit of donated or drawn blood, not a pregnancy.
whyWrongByChoice.E: 1500-2000 mg overstates the per-pregnancy loss described in the source.

questions.stem: A 7-year-old boy presents for follow-up. His mother mentions that he receives red blood cell transfusions once per month for treatment of his anemia, and that his aunt and cousin require similar treatments. Which of the six key history questions is most directly illustrated by this vignette?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Is the anemia inherited or acquired?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Is there evidence of blood loss?

choices.label: E
choices.text: Has the patient been exposed to medication or toxins?

explanation.summary: Young age at onset, chronic transfusion dependence, and a positive family history point to the question of whether the anemia is inherited or acquired.
explanation.whyCorrect: Inherited causes of anemia typically present at an earlier age, and a family history of relatives requiring treatment or transfusions suggests a genetic process. Transfusions given at a young age without an obvious acquired cause are a specific clue to an inherited anemia.

whyWrongByChoice.B: No source of bleeding such as melena, heavy menses, or hematuria is described in the vignette.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The child clearly has true anemia, since he requires monthly transfusions, so a dilutional cause is not in question.
whyWrongByChoice.D: No jaundice, dark urine, or splenomegaly is described to raise the question of hemolysis specifically.
whyWrongByChoice.E: No relevant drug or toxin exposure is mentioned that would implicate a medication-induced anemia.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man is evaluated for fatigue. On examination he has scleral icterus, and he reports his urine has been dark. His spleen is mildly enlarged. Laboratory studies show an elevated indirect (unconjugated) bilirubin. Which of the following processes best explains this clinical picture?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Premature destruction of red blood cells

choices.label: D
choices.text: Impaired DNA synthesis in red cell precursors

explanation.summary: Jaundice with elevated indirect bilirubin, dark urine, and splenomegaly are characteristic signs of hemolysis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Bilirubin is a breakdown product of heme; unconjugated (indirect) bilirubin rises with the increased red cell turnover of hemolysis, causing jaundice. Dark urine reflects hemoglobinuria when hemolysis is intravascular, and the spleen, a common site of red cell destruction, can enlarge with chronic hemolysis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Plasma volume expansion causes a dilutional anemia and does not raise bilirubin or produce jaundice.
whyWrongByChoice.C: GI blood loss causes iron deficiency and does not elevate indirect bilirubin or cause jaundice and dark urine.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A maturation defect from impaired DNA synthesis causes megaloblastic anemia, not the jaundice-and-dark-urine picture of hemolysis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Marrow aplasia reduces cell production and does not generate the increased red cell turnover that raises indirect bilirubin.

questions.stem: A previously healthy 25-year-old man develops fatigue, pallor, and jaundice a few days after starting primaquine for malaria prophylaxis. Laboratory studies are consistent with hemolytic anemia. A deficiency of which of the following enzymes best explains his susceptibility to this oxidant drug?

explanation.summary: G6PD deficiency leaves red cells unable to detoxify reactive oxygen species, so oxidant drugs trigger hemolysis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Patients with G6PD deficiency lack the enzyme needed to detoxify hydrogen peroxide, making red cells susceptible to oxidant stress. The classic presentation is a previously healthy person who, after an oxidant stressor such as primaquine, dapsone, or sulfa drugs, develops signs of hemolytic anemia (fatigue, pallor, jaundice).

whyWrongByChoice.B: Pyruvate kinase deficiency causes a chronic nonspherocytic hemolytic anemia but is not the enzyme defect triggered by oxidant drugs described here.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Dihydrofolate reductase is inhibited by folate antagonists such as methotrexate, which cause a maturation-defect anemia, not oxidant hemolysis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Glucocerebrosidase deficiency causes Gaucher disease, a lysosomal storage disorder, not oxidant-induced hemolysis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Adenosine deaminase deficiency causes severe combined immunodeficiency, not drug-induced hemolytic anemia.

questions.stem: A physician is reviewing a patient's medications while evaluating a new anemia. She notes the patient takes ibuprofen frequently for chronic pain. Through which mechanism is this medication most likely to contribute to anemia?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Folate antagonism impairing red cell maturation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Direct suppression of the bone marrow

explanation.summary: NSAIDs such as ibuprofen classically cause anemia through gastrointestinal blood loss.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick lists non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs, like ibuprofen) as agents that cause GI blood loss. Chronic GI bleeding depletes iron and produces iron deficiency anemia over time.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Oxidant hemolysis is caused by drugs like dapsone, sulfa drugs, and primaquine, not by NSAIDs.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Folate antagonism causing a maturation defect is the mechanism of trimethoprim and methotrexate, not ibuprofen.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Marrow suppression is associated with chemotherapy, insecticides, and anticonvulsants such as phenytoin, not NSAIDs.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Plasma volume expansion causes dilutional anemia and is unrelated to NSAID use.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with long-standing chronic kidney disease is found to be anemic. He has no evidence of bleeding, hemolysis, or a dilutional cause, and takes no marrow-toxic medications. Which of the six key history questions best captures the most likely explanation for his anemia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Is there evidence of a systemic illness or non-hematologic organ dysfunction?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Is there evidence of blood loss?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Is the anemia inherited or acquired?

explanation.summary: Renal disease is a non-hematologic organ dysfunction that can cause anemia, captured by the systemic-illness history question.
explanation.whyCorrect: Many underlying conditions, including infection, chronic inflammation, renal disease, liver disease, endocrine disease, and malignancy, can be associated with anemia. Chronic kidney disease is a classic example of a non-hematologic organ dysfunction that produces anemia.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The vignette explicitly states there is no evidence of bleeding, so a blood-loss source is not the explanation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: There is no jaundice, dark urine, or splenomegaly, and the stem states there is no hemolysis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Onset in later adulthood with a clear organ cause argues against an inherited anemia presenting at a young age.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A dilutional cause is excluded by the stem, so the patient is truly anemic.

questions.stem: A physician is reviewing a panel of patients to determine who is at lowest risk for developing iron-deficiency anemia. Which of the following patients has the LEAST risk for iron deficiency?

choices.label: D
choices.text: A patient with peptic ulcer disease taking large doses of oral antacids

choices.label: E
choices.text: A pregnant woman who follows a vegan diet

explanation.summary: Iron-deficiency anemia is driven by blood loss, poor intake/absorption, or increased demand; a postmenopausal woman with no symptoms lacks all of these.
explanation.whyCorrect: Women are at risk for iron deficiency during pregnancy (increased demand) and during their reproductive years (menstrual blood loss). After menopause, menstrual losses stop, so an asymptomatic postmenopausal woman has the lowest risk of the options listed.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A 60-year-old man with heme-positive stools is losing blood from the GI tract, a classic cause of iron deficiency that should raise concern for malignancy.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A premenopausal woman loses iron through ongoing menstrual bleeding, the most common overall cause of iron deficiency.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Chronic high-dose antacids reduce GI iron absorption, promoting iron deficiency.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pregnancy sharply raises iron demand, and a vegan diet supplies only poorly absorbed nonheme iron, compounding the risk.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman reports several months of fatigue and decreased exercise tolerance. She has four children and a full-time job. Her only notable finding on review of systems is heavy menstrual periods. Vital signs and physical exam are unremarkable. Laboratory studies show a microcytic, hypochromic anemia with low serum ferritin. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of her anemia?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Impaired iron absorption in the duodenum

explanation.summary: Heavy menses is the most common overall cause of iron-deficiency anemia in premenopausal women.
explanation.whyCorrect: Menstrual bleeding causes roughly 1 mg of iron loss per day, and heavy menses in a premenopausal woman is the single most common cause of iron-deficiency anemia. Her low ferritin and microcytic, hypochromic indices fit chronic blood loss from menstruation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: GI malignancy is the leading concern in an older man or postmenopausal woman, not a menstruating 32-year-old with an obvious bleeding source.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Impaired absorption occurs with malabsorption syndromes or bariatric surgery, none of which are present here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Chronic intravascular hemolysis (e.g., a malfunctioning heart valve or PNH) is an uncommon cause and is not suggested by her history.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Anemia of chronic inflammation typically shows normal or elevated ferritin, not the low ferritin seen here.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman with long-standing rheumatoid arthritis is evaluated for a microcytic anemia thought to be due to iron deficiency. Because of her active inflammatory disease, some iron studies may be misleading. Which of the following laboratory values would be abnormally LOW in this patient?

explanation.summary: Serum iron is low in iron deficiency, but is nondiagnostic alone because it is also low in anemia of chronic disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: Serum iron is decreased in iron-deficiency anemia. Because it is also decreased in anemia of chronic disease/inflammation, it is the low value expected here, though it is nonspecific by itself.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The red cell distribution width is increased in iron deficiency because red cells vary in size (anisocytosis).
whyWrongByChoice.B: Ferritin is a positive acute-phase reactant; in a patient with active rheumatoid arthritis it can be normal or elevated, masking iron deficiency.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Total iron-binding capacity rises in iron deficiency as the liver makes more transferrin.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hepcidin is increased by inflammatory cytokines, not decreased, and helps drive the anemia of chronic disease.

questions.stem: A 67-year-old man presents with 3 months of progressive fatigue, shortness of breath, and intermittent lightheadedness. Exam is notable for conjunctival pallor and a positive stool hemoccult test. Complete blood count shows hemoglobin 8.7 g/dL with a low mean corpuscular volume, and serum ferritin is 10 ng/mL. In addition to starting oral iron, which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

explanation.summary: Iron-deficiency anemia with occult GI bleeding in an older man requires evaluation for a GI malignancy such as colon cancer.
explanation.whyCorrect: This man has microcytic anemia with low ferritin and a positive hemoccult test. Iron deficiency in an older man is due to GI malignancy until proven otherwise, so the appropriate next step is colonoscopy (with upper GI endoscopy if indicated).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Abdominal ultrasound is used when hemoperitoneum is suspected in trauma; such patients are acutely symptomatic and unstable.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Transfusion is reserved for severe or life-threatening anemia or hemodynamic instability, not this stable patient.
whyWrongByChoice.D: CT of the abdomen is used to evaluate suspected infection or inflammation of the GI tract and nearby organs, not to localize occult bleeding here.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hemoglobin electrophoresis is used to evaluate suspected thalassemia, which is unlikely given the low ferritin and positive hemoccult.

questions.stem: A researcher is studying how iron deficiency reduces hemoglobin production. Normally, the final step of heme synthesis inserts iron into a ring-shaped precursor molecule. Impairment of which of the following steps best explains the decreased heme production in iron-deficiency anemia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Insertion of iron into protoporphyrin by ferrochelatase

choices.label: B
choices.text: Oxidation of ferrous iron to ferric iron by hephaestin

choices.label: C
choices.text: Binding of iron to transferrin in the plasma

choices.label: D
choices.text: Reduction of ferric iron to ferrous iron in the gut lumen

choices.label: E
choices.text: Storage of iron within ferritin in the liver

explanation.summary: Heme synthesis ends with ferrochelatase inserting iron into protoporphyrin; iron deficiency starves this step.
explanation.whyCorrect: Heme is the iron-containing component of hemoglobin. Its final synthetic step is the insertion of iron into the protoporphyrin molecule by ferrochelatase. When iron is deficient, this step fails, decreasing heme, then hemoglobin, and eventually the number of red cells.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Hephaestin oxidizes iron during absorption/export from enterocytes; it is not the rate-limiting step of heme synthesis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Transferrin binding is a transport function in blood, not a step of the heme biosynthetic pathway.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Luminal reduction of ferric to ferrous iron aids intestinal absorption but is upstream of heme synthesis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ferritin storage sequesters iron; it is not part of assembling the heme molecule.

questions.stem: During infection and inflammation, the body sequesters iron inside cells to keep it away from pathogens. Which of the following molecules causes iron to remain sequestered in cells by degrading ferroportin, thereby preventing iron from entering the bloodstream?

explanation.summary: Hepcidin blocks iron export by causing internalization and degradation of ferroportin.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hepcidin, secreted by hepatocytes and increased by inflammatory cytokines, causes ferroportin to be internalized and degraded. Without functioning ferroportin, iron cannot leave enterocytes, macrophages, or hepatocytes and remains sequestered inside cells.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Ferritin is the intracellular storage protein for iron; it packs iron away but does not act on ferroportin.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Erythropoietin actually inhibits hepatic hepcidin production, promoting iron release rather than sequestration.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Transferrin transports iron through the bloodstream; it does not regulate ferroportin.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Ferroportin is the transport protein that is degraded by hepcidin, not the molecule causing sequestration.

questions.stem: A previously healthy person relocates to a high-altitude region where tissue oxygen levels are chronically low. Over the following weeks, the kidney increases production of a hormone that stimulates red blood cell production and simultaneously enhances delivery of iron to the marrow. Through which of the following mechanisms does this hormone increase circulating iron?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Stimulating hepcidin release to degrade ferroportin

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increasing ferritin synthesis to store more iron in cells

choices.label: D
choices.text: Decreasing transferrin production in the liver

choices.label: E
choices.text: Blocking heme carrier protein 1 in the duodenum

explanation.summary: Erythropoietin inhibits hepcidin, freeing ferroportin to move more iron into the blood for new red cells.
explanation.whyCorrect: When tissue oxygen is low, the kidney releases erythropoietin, which stimulates the marrow to make red cells and also inhibits hepatic hepcidin production. Less hepcidin means ferroportin is preserved, so more iron is transported into the bloodstream for erythropoiesis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Stimulating hepcidin would degrade ferroportin and trap iron in cells, the opposite of what is needed for red cell production.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Increasing ferritin storage would keep iron inside cells rather than raising circulating iron.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Erythropoietin does not work by lowering transferrin; transferrin actually carries the mobilized iron.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Blocking HCP1 would impair intestinal iron absorption, reducing rather than increasing available iron.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old child is brought in by a parent who is worried because the child repeatedly chews on ice cubes and has been caught eating dirt and paint chips. The child appears pale. A complete blood count shows a microcytic, hypochromic anemia. This child's unusual eating behavior is most specifically associated with which of the following?

explanation.summary: Pica, the craving of nonfood items, is a symptom specifically linked to iron deficiency.
explanation.whyCorrect: Persistent ingestion of nonfood items such as ice, dirt, clay, or paint chips is termed pica and is a symptom specifically related to iron deficiency. This child's microcytic, hypochromic anemia supports iron-deficiency anemia.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Reactive thrombocytosis can accompany iron deficiency but is a laboratory finding, not the cause of the behavior.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The microcytic indices point to iron deficiency; B12 deficiency causes a macrocytic anemia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Chronic inflammation causes anemia of chronic disease but is not the classic cause of pica.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hemolysis is not associated with pica and typically does not produce microcytic, hypochromic cells.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with a long history of heavy menstrual bleeding is evaluated for chronic fatigue. On examination, her fingernails curve upward into a concave, spoon-like shape, and there are cracks at the corners of her mouth. Which of the following best describes the spoon-shaped nail finding?

explanation.summary: Koilonychia is the spoon-shaped, upwardly concave nail seen in chronic iron deficiency.
explanation.whyCorrect: Koilonychia describes a condition in which the nails curve upward into a concave, spoon-like shape and is a physical finding suggestive of iron-deficiency anemia. Her heavy menses and fatigue fit chronic iron deficiency.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Angular cheilitis refers to the cracks and fissures at the corners of the mouth, not the nail change.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Atrophic glossitis involves shrinkage of the tongue papillae, producing a shiny, smooth tongue.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Conjunctival pallor is a nonspecific sign of anemia affecting the mucous membranes, not the nails.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Glossitis is inflammation and enlargement of the tongue, unrelated to the nail finding.

questions.stem: A 29-year-old vegetarian woman with fatigue has a complete blood count showing a low hemoglobin and low mean corpuscular volume. A peripheral blood smear is reviewed. Which of the following sets of findings is most consistent with iron-deficiency anemia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hypochromic red cells with increased central pallor and anisocytosis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Macrocytic red cells with hypersegmented neutrophils

choices.label: C
choices.text: Spherocytes with no central pallor and polychromasia

choices.label: D
choices.text: Normocytic, normochromic red cells with rouleaux formation

choices.label: E
choices.text: Schistocytes and helmet cells with thrombocytopenia

explanation.summary: The iron-deficiency smear shows small, pale (hypochromic) cells with increased central pallor and size variation.
explanation.whyCorrect: In iron-deficiency anemia the red cells are hypochromic (abnormally pale) because of decreased hemoglobin content, and the zone of central pallor is increased beyond the normal one-third of cell diameter. Anisocytosis (variation in size) is also seen, matching the elevated RDW.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Macrocytic cells with hypersegmented neutrophils indicate a megaloblastic anemia (B12 or folate deficiency).
whyWrongByChoice.C: Spherocytes lacking central pallor with polychromasia point to hemolysis, such as hereditary spherocytosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Normocytic, normochromic cells with rouleaux suggest a different process, not the microcytic iron-deficiency picture.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Schistocytes and helmet cells with thrombocytopenia indicate a microangiopathic hemolytic process, not iron deficiency.

questions.stem: A patient with iron-deficiency anemia has iron studies drawn. The total iron-binding capacity is elevated. Which of the following best explains why the total iron-binding capacity rises in iron deficiency?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ferritin is released from the liver into the plasma

choices.label: D
choices.text: More binding sites on transferrin become occupied by iron

explanation.summary: TIBC measures available transferrin binding sites, which rise as the liver makes more transferrin in iron deficiency.
explanation.whyCorrect: Total iron-binding capacity reflects the number of open iron-binding sites on circulating transferrin. In iron deficiency, the liver produces more transferrin to try to capture the limited iron; because there is little iron and more transferrin, the TIBC goes up.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Ferritin is a storage protein, and its level actually falls in iron deficiency; it does not determine TIBC.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hepcidin degradation of ferroportin controls iron export, not the transferrin binding capacity measured by TIBC.
whyWrongByChoice.D: More occupied binding sites would raise transferrin saturation, which is actually low in iron deficiency; TIBC reflects open sites.
