whyWrongByChoice.E: Erythropoietin from the kidney increases with anemia and does not account for the elevated TIBC.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old woman with severe Crohn disease and documented iron-deficiency anemia cannot tolerate oral iron because it worsens her gastrointestinal symptoms, and her intestinal disease impairs absorption. Which of the following is the most appropriate route of iron replacement for this patient?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Oral ferrous sulfate at a higher dose

choices.label: E
choices.text: Oral iron combined with an antacid

explanation.summary: Intravenous iron is used for patients who cannot tolerate or absorb oral iron, such as those with inflammatory bowel disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: Intravenous iron is given to patients who cannot tolerate or receive oral iron and to those who cannot absorb it, such as patients with inflammatory bowel disease, malabsorption syndromes, or prior gastric bypass. This patient meets both criteria.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Higher-dose oral iron will still cause GI intolerance and will not be absorbed adequately given her intestinal disease.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Transfusion is reserved for severe or life-threatening anemia, not routine iron replacement in a stable patient.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Dietary counseling alone cannot correct an established deficiency in a patient with malabsorption.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Antacids reduce GI iron absorption, so combining them with oral iron would worsen the problem.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman is found to have a hemoglobin of 11.2 g/dL and a mean corpuscular volume of 68 fL on routine testing. She is asymptomatic. Her red blood cell count is elevated, her red cell distribution width is normal, and her serum ferritin is normal. Which of the following findings most reliably distinguishes her condition from iron-deficiency anemia?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Normal to increased red blood cell count

choices.label: D
choices.text: Presence of hypochromia on the smear

explanation.summary: In mild thalassemia the RBC count is normal or increased and the RDW is normal, unlike iron deficiency.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick emphasizes that although hemoglobin is low in mild thalassemia, the RBC count is usually normal or increased, whereas it is low in iron-deficiency anemia. This is a key distinguishing clue in the CBC, along with a normal RDW.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Both thalassemia trait and iron deficiency produce a low MCV (microcytosis), so this does not separate them.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Low hemoglobin occurs in both conditions and does not distinguish them.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hypochromia is seen in both microcytic anemias and is not discriminating.
whyWrongByChoice.E: An elevated RDW favors iron deficiency; in thalassemia minor the RDW is typically normal.

questions.stem: A patient of Southeast Asian descent has moderate to severe microcytic anemia. Genetic testing shows a genotype in which three of the four alpha-globin genes are deleted, and hemoglobin analysis reveals an unstable tetramer composed of four beta chains. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Deletion of three alpha-globin genes produces HbH disease, with excess beta chains forming HbH (beta-4) tetramers.
explanation.whyCorrect: Per Table 1, deletion of three alpha genes (a-/--) causes HbH disease with moderate to severe anemia. The excess unpaired beta chains aggregate into HbH tetramers, which are insoluble, cannot carry oxygen, and shorten RBC lifespan.

whyWrongByChoice.A: The silent carrier has only one alpha gene deleted and is asymptomatic with no anemia.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Alpha-thalassemia trait involves two deleted alpha genes and causes only mild anemia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hydrops fetalis results from deletion of all four alpha genes and is usually fatal in utero, forming Hb Barts (gamma-4), not HbH.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Beta-thalassemia major is caused by beta-globin mutations, not alpha-gene deletions.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old man has been chronically jaundiced and anemic since childhood and has required transfusions for repeated episodes of severe anemia. Examination shows splenomegaly, frontal bossing, and prominent malar eminences. The CBC shows a microcytic, hypochromic anemia, and hemoglobin electrophoresis shows elevated HbA2 and HbF with undetectable HbA. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Undetectable HbA with transfusion dependence and skeletal changes indicates beta-thalassemia major (beta-0/beta-0).
explanation.whyCorrect: The transfusion requirement, severe anemia, splenomegaly, and bossing from extramedullary hematopoiesis, combined with electrophoresis showing no HbA, point to transfusion-dependent beta-thalassemia. With no beta chains produced, HbF and HbA2 rise to compensate.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Alpha-thalassemia trait is usually asymptomatic with a normal hemoglobin electrophoresis.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Beta-thalassemia trait is asymptomatic and shows low-normal HbA with only mildly elevated HbA2 and HbF, not absent HbA.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hemoglobin Bart disease is incompatible with life and causes intrauterine fetal demise.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hemoglobin H disease shows elevated HbH with low HbA, HbA2, and HbF, not the pattern described.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old with severe beta-thalassemia has received regular red blood cell transfusions for many years. He now develops a cardiomyopathy with heart failure and abnormal liver function tests. Which of the following is the most likely complication responsible for these findings?

explanation.summary: Chronic transfusion causes iron overload, depositing iron in the heart, liver, and endocrine organs.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick states that frequent transfusions increase the risk of iron overload, or secondary hemochromatosis, leading to iron deposition in the heart, liver, and endocrine organs, with complications such as heart failure, arrhythmias, and liver disease. Iron chelators like deferoxamine are given to prevent this.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Graft-versus-host disease is a complication of stem cell transplant, not routine transfusion.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Transfusion-transmitted hepatitis C is rare in developed countries because of adequate blood screening.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Transfusion-transmitted HIV is likewise rare due to blood screening and does not explain the iron-loading pattern.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hemolysis from a mismatched transfusion is very rare and would not cause chronic organ iron deposition.

questions.stem: A newborn with a genetically confirmed severe form of beta-thalassemia major appears healthy at birth and remains asymptomatic for the first several months of life, developing anemia only around 6 to 12 months of age. Which of the following best explains the delayed onset of symptoms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Fetal hemoglobin contains alpha and gamma chains and can deliver oxygen adequately

choices.label: B
choices.text: Maternal HbA crosses the placenta and persists for the first year

choices.label: C
choices.text: Beta chain synthesis is maximal at birth and declines slowly

choices.label: D
choices.text: Newborns compensate by producing excess HbA2 (alpha-2 delta-2)

choices.label: E
choices.text: Iron stores from birth prevent anemia until they are depleted

explanation.summary: HbF (alpha-2 gamma-2) does not require beta chains and sustains oxygen delivery until it declines after birth.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick explains that fetal hemoglobin (HbF) contains alpha and gamma globin and does not contain beta globin, so it is unaffected by beta-thalassemia. HbF persists for at least 6 months after birth and delivers oxygen adequately; children become symptomatic only as HbF levels fall.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The brick attributes protection to the newborn's own HbF, not to persistent maternal hemoglobin.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Beta chain synthesis is defective in beta-thalassemia and rises after birth as gamma falls, which is when symptoms appear.
whyWrongByChoice.D: HbA2 is only a minor hemoglobin and is not the reason neonates are protected.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Iron stores are not the protective factor; the anemia is due to defective globin production, not iron deficiency.

questions.stem: A fetus is found on prenatal evaluation to have severe, diffuse edema and profound anemia, and intrauterine demise occurs. Molecular studies show deletion of all four alpha-globin genes. Which abnormal hemoglobin is most likely responsible for this presentation?

explanation.summary: With no alpha chains, fetal gamma chains form Hb Barts (gamma-4), causing hydrops fetalis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Deletion of all four alpha genes prevents alpha chain production. In the fetus, the unpaired gamma chains that normally partner with alpha chains clump into tetramers of four gamma chains called Hb Barts. This causes extremely severe anemia, diffuse edema, and usually intrauterine death (hydrops fetalis).

whyWrongByChoice.A: HbH (beta-4) forms in adults with three-gene alpha deletion, not in the alpha-null fetus.
whyWrongByChoice.C: HbA2 is a minor normal hemoglobin and requires alpha chains, which are absent here.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hemoglobin S is a structural beta-chain variant unrelated to alpha-gene deletions.
whyWrongByChoice.E: HbA requires alpha chains, which cannot be made when all four alpha genes are deleted.

questions.stem: A 9-year-old child with untreated severe thalassemia has a depressed nasal bridge, maxillary hypertrophy, prominent malar eminences, exposed upper teeth, and frontal bossing. Hepatosplenomegaly is also present. These findings are best explained by which of the following processes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Iron deposition in bone and soft tissue

choices.label: B
choices.text: Repeated microvascular occlusion by sickled cells

choices.label: D
choices.text: Chronic hypoxic injury to growth plates

explanation.summary: Ineffective erythropoiesis drives extramedullary hematopoiesis and marrow hyperplasia, expanding facial and skull bones and enlarging liver and spleen.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because of ineffective erythropoiesis, patients with severe thalassemia make RBCs at extramedullary sites such as the spleen and liver (causing hepatosplenomegaly) and in the bones of the skull and face. Marrow hyperplasia produces the depressed nasal bridge, maxillary hypertrophy, prominent malar eminence, exposed upper teeth, and frontal bossing described.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Iron overload harms the heart, liver, and endocrine organs but does not cause the described skeletal marrow expansion.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Vaso-occlusion is a feature of sickle cell disease, not the marrow-expansion changes of thalassemia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The facial and skull changes result from marrow hyperplasia, not hypoxic growth-plate injury.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Thalassemia is a genetic globin-production disorder, not an autoimmune anemia.

questions.stem: A hematologist explains that the two broad categories of beta-globin gene mutations in beta-thalassemia are designated beta-0 and beta-plus. Which of the following correctly describes the beta-0 mutation?

choices.label: B
choices.text: No beta chain production from the affected gene

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reduced but present beta chain production

choices.label: D
choices.text: Production of a structurally abnormal beta chain

explanation.summary: Beta-0 mutations produce no beta chains, whereas beta-plus mutations produce some but not enough.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick states that the two broad categories of mutations in beta-thalassemia are those that result in no beta chain production (designated beta-0) and those that result in some, but not enough, beta chain production (beta-plus). A beta-0/beta-0 genotype produces transfusion-dependent thalassemia major.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Beta-thalassemia is caused by non-deletional mutations, not gene deletions; alpha-thalassemia is the deletional disorder.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Reduced but present production describes the beta-plus mutation, not beta-0.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A structurally abnormal chain describes a structural hemoglobinopathy (e.g., HbS), not the quantitative beta-0 defect.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Beta-thalassemia results from mutations, not deletions, of the two chromosome 11 beta genes.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old asymptomatic man has a mild microcytic anemia with a normal serum ferritin. Hemoglobin electrophoresis is ordered and shows an elevated HbA2 with a slightly increased HbF. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: An elevated HbA2 on electrophoresis in a person with mild microcytic anemia indicates beta-thalassemia trait.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hemoglobin electrophoresis is often adequate to diagnose beta-thalassemias. When beta chain output is reduced, other hemoglobins rise to compensate; in beta-thalassemia trait, HbA2 rises (up to about 5%) with mildly elevated HbF, producing mild anemia in an otherwise asymptomatic patient.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Alpha-thalassemia trait shows a normal hemoglobin electrophoresis, not elevated HbA2.
whyWrongByChoice.B: The silent carrier state has no anemia and a normal electrophoresis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Iron deficiency does not raise HbA2, and this patient's ferritin is normal.
whyWrongByChoice.E: HbH disease shows decreased HbA with detectable HbH and causes moderate to severe anemia, not this mild picture.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman with transfusion-dependent beta-thalassemia is started on a drug intended to reduce her transfusion requirement. The medication works by binding transforming growth factor-beta (TGF-beta) ligands on erythroid precursor cells in the bone marrow, thereby enhancing erythroid maturation. Which of the following agents was most likely prescribed?

explanation.summary: Luspatercept binds TGF-beta ligands on erythroid precursors to enhance erythroid maturation and reduce transfusion need.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick describes that transfusion needs can sometimes be reduced using luspatercept, which increases bone marrow RBC production by binding transforming growth factor-beta ligands on erythroid precursor cells, leading to enhanced erythroid maturation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Deferoxamine is an iron chelator used to prevent iron overload, not to stimulate erythroid maturation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Erythropoietin stimulates precursor proliferation via the EPO receptor, not by binding TGF-beta ligands.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hydroxyurea raises HbF in some hemoglobinopathies but is not the TGF-beta ligand trap described here.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Prednisone is a corticosteroid and is not the described mechanism of enhancing erythroid maturation in thalassemia.

questions.stem: A researcher explains why reduced alpha or beta chain synthesis leads to anemia in thalassemia. Which of the following best describes the underlying pathophysiology?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Unbound globin chains precipitate within developing RBCs

choices.label: B
choices.text: A structurally abnormal globin chain polymerizes and deforms RBCs into a sickle shape

choices.label: C
choices.text: Antibodies coat RBCs and trigger their destruction in the spleen

choices.label: D
choices.text: Impaired heme synthesis leads to iron accumulation in mitochondria

choices.label: E
choices.text: A membrane cytoskeletal defect causes spherocyte formation

explanation.summary: Chain imbalance leaves free globin chains that precipitate in RBCs, causing ineffective erythropoiesis and hemolysis.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick teaches that reduced alpha or beta chain synthesis disrupts the tightly controlled 1.0 alpha-to-beta ratio, leaving free unbound chains that precipitate within the developing RBC and damage it. This causes reduced RBC development (ineffective erythropoiesis) and early RBC death, plus membrane changes that promote lysis in the circulation (hemolysis).

whyWrongByChoice.B: Polymerization of an abnormal chain into a sickle shape describes sickle cell disease, a structural hemoglobinopathy, not thalassemia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Antibody-mediated destruction describes autoimmune hemolytic anemia, not thalassemia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Iron accumulation in mitochondria describes sideroblastic anemia, not thalassemia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A cytoskeletal membrane defect causing spherocytes describes hereditary spherocytosis.

questions.stem: A 3-year-old child with severe beta-thalassemia develops worsening cytopenias, massive symptomatic splenomegaly, and a rising transfusion requirement. His physicians consider a procedure to reduce transfusion needs and improve the cytopenias. Which of the following interventions is most appropriate for these specific findings?

explanation.summary: Splenectomy is appropriate for hypersplenism causing cytopenias and rising transfusion needs with symptomatic splenomegaly.
explanation.whyCorrect: The brick notes that splenectomy may be appropriate for individuals with severe thalassemia who have findings such as a dramatic increase in transfusion requirements, hypersplenism leading to other cytopenias, and symptomatic splenomegaly. Removing the spleen reduces cell trapping and transfusion needs.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Deferoxamine treats iron overload but does not address the hypersplenism and splenomegaly driving these findings.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Iron supplementation is inappropriate and can worsen the iron overload common in these patients.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Observation is inadequate given the dramatic rise in transfusion requirement and symptomatic splenomegaly.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Erythropoietin is not the described intervention for hypersplenism and symptomatic splenomegaly.

questions.stem: A 43-year-old woman with a 5-year history of rheumatoid arthritis reports 3 months of worsening fatigue and pallor. Her hemoglobin is 10.2 g/dL with a mean corpuscular volume of 88 fL (normal). Iron studies show a low serum iron, a low total iron-binding capacity, a normal transferrin saturation, and an elevated ferritin. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: A normocytic anemia with low serum iron, low TIBC, and high ferritin in a patient with a chronic inflammatory disease is anemia of chronic disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: In anemia of chronic disease (ACD), inflammation raises hepcidin, trapping iron in macrophages and hepatocytes. This produces low serum iron with a paradoxically normal or high ferritin (a positive acute-phase reactant) and a low TIBC (transferrin is a negative acute-phase reactant). The link to rheumatoid arthritis and the normocytic indices fit ACD.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Iron-deficiency anemia shows a LOW ferritin and a HIGH TIBC, the opposite of this pattern.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Thalassemia minor gives microcytosis with normal or high iron studies and a normal/high RBC count, not iron sequestration tied to inflammation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Sideroblastic anemia typically shows high serum iron and high ferritin with ring sideroblasts, not a low serum iron.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Vitamin B12 deficiency causes a macrocytic (high MCV) anemia, not a normocytic anemia with these iron studies.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman with systemic lupus erythematosus presents with fatigue and mild shortness of breath when climbing stairs. Her complete blood count shows a hemoglobin of 10.0 g/dL. Increased synthesis of which of the following most likely led to this anemia?

explanation.summary: Inflammatory cytokines drive hepcidin production, which sequesters iron and causes anemia of chronic disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: In chronic inflammatory disease such as lupus, high IL-6 increases hepcidin. Hepcidin degrades ferroportin-1, reducing iron release from macrophages and hepatocytes and iron transfer from enterocytes, so less iron reaches the marrow to make new RBCs.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Erythropoietin rises in response to anemia; it is an effect, not the cause of ACD (and is not the driver except in chronic kidney disease).
whyWrongByChoice.B: Haptoglobin binds free hemoglobin after intravascular hemolysis and falls in hemolytic anemia; it is normal in ACD.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hypoxia-inducible factor stimulates erythropoietin and RBC production in any anemia; it does not cause ACD.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Protoporphyrin is a heme component; an increase in it does not cause ACD.

questions.stem: In a patient with anemia of chronic disease/inflammation, an increased value in which of the following serum tests is most characteristic?

explanation.summary: Ferritin is a positive acute-phase reactant and is normal or high in ACD.
explanation.whyCorrect: During inflammation the liver makes more ferritin, so it is normal or increased in ACD. A high ferritin is the key value that distinguishes ACD from iron-deficiency anemia, in which ferritin is low.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Serum iron is LOW in ACD because iron is trapped in macrophages and hepatocytes.
whyWrongByChoice.C: TIBC is LOW because transferrin is a negative acute-phase reactant.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Transferrin saturation is most often low, sometimes normal, but not increased.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Transferrin (measured as TIBC) is LOW, since the liver makes less of it during inflammation.

questions.stem: Anemia of chronic disease is caused primarily by a decrease in which of the following?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Excretion of iron in the urine

choices.label: D
choices.text: Storage of iron in the bone marrow

choices.label: E
choices.text: Uptake of intestinal iron by enterocytes

explanation.summary: Increased hepcidin blocks iron release from macrophages and hepatocytes, limiting iron for RBC production.
explanation.whyCorrect: In ACD, hepcidin degrades ferroportin-1 so macrophages and hepatocytes cannot release stored iron. Less circulating iron reaches the marrow, limiting incorporation into RBC precursors.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Urinary iron excretion does not play a role in the pathogenesis of ACD.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Oxidation of iron within hemoglobin is not a mechanism of ACD.
whyWrongByChoice.D: There is no increased marrow iron storage; iron cannot even reach the marrow because it is trapped in hepatocytes and macrophages.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Enterocyte uptake of intestinal iron is normal; the problem is that iron cannot exit the enterocyte into plasma.

questions.stem: A 26-year-old woman with systemic lupus erythematosus, treated with several medications, presents for follow-up. Her complete blood count shows a hemoglobin of 10.0 g/dL (normal 12.0-16.0) with a mean corpuscular volume of 90 fL (normal 80-100). She has only mild symptoms. Which of the following is the most appropriate management of her anemia?

explanation.summary: Management of ACD focuses on treating the underlying disorder, not on directly treating the anemia in mild cases.
explanation.whyCorrect: Treatment of ACD is aimed at the underlying disease; controlling the inflammation typically resolves the anemia. In mild, normocytic anemia without documented iron deficiency, no specific anemia-directed therapy is required.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Oral iron is warranted only if a concurrent iron deficiency is documented with iron studies.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Erythropoietin is generally reserved for severe cases, not mild ACD.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Transfusion is not indicated until anemia is profound, typically below 7 g/dL.
whyWrongByChoice.E: IV iron is generally not required for mild ACD and is reserved for more severe cases.

questions.stem: In chronic inflammation, hepcidin lowers plasma iron primarily through which of the following mechanisms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increasing transferrin synthesis by the liver

choices.label: B
choices.text: Causing internalization and degradation of ferroportin-1

choices.label: C
choices.text: Enhancing iron absorption across the duodenal brush border

choices.label: D
choices.text: Stimulating erythropoietin release from the kidney

choices.label: E
choices.text: Directly lysing red blood cell precursors in the marrow

explanation.summary: Hepcidin degrades the iron exporter ferroportin-1, trapping iron inside cells.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hepcidin triggers internalization and degradation of ferroportin-1. Decreased ferroportin blocks iron release from enterocytes into plasma and from macrophages and hepatocytes, lowering circulating iron.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Transferrin (TIBC) actually falls in inflammation because it is a negative acute-phase reactant; hepcidin does not increase it.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hepcidin inhibits, rather than enhances, iron transfer from the enterocyte into the plasma.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hepcidin acts on iron transport, not on renal erythropoietin release.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hepcidin controls iron handling; it does not directly lyse RBC precursors.

questions.stem: A patient with a chronic inflammatory condition develops anemia of chronic disease. Which of the following inflammatory cytokines most directly increases hepcidin production by the liver?

explanation.summary: IL-6 is the key inflammatory cytokine that drives hepcidin production.
explanation.whyCorrect: In chronic inflammation, high levels of IL-6 increase hepatic hepcidin production, which in turn sequesters iron and produces anemia of chronic disease.

whyWrongByChoice.B: IL-10 is an anti-inflammatory cytokine and is not the driver of hepcidin in ACD.
whyWrongByChoice.C: TGF-beta is not described as the cytokine that raises hepcidin in this setting.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Erythropoietin is a hormone that stimulates RBC production; increased erythropoietic activity actually suppresses hepcidin.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Intrinsic factor is required for vitamin B12 absorption and has no role in hepcidin regulation.

questions.stem: A patient with anemia of chronic disease has a low serum iron but an elevated ferritin. Which of the following best explains the elevated ferritin?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ferritin is a positive acute-phase reactant

choices.label: B
choices.text: Total body iron stores are depleted

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ongoing intravascular hemolysis is releasing iron

choices.label: D
choices.text: The kidney is failing to excrete ferritin

explanation.summary: Ferritin rises in inflammation because it is a positive acute-phase reactant.
explanation.whyCorrect: The liver produces more ferritin during inflammation, so ferritin is normal or high in ACD even though usable circulating iron is low. This high ferritin distinguishes ACD from iron-deficiency anemia.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Depleted iron stores would produce a LOW ferritin, as seen in iron-deficiency anemia, not a high one.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hemolysis is not the mechanism of the high ferritin in ACD; the iron is sequestered, not released by RBC destruction.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ferritin is not elevated because of impaired renal excretion; it reflects increased synthesis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Transferrin saturation is typically low or normal in ACD, not elevated.

questions.stem: In addition to decreased erythrocyte production, which of the following additional mechanisms contributes to the anemia of chronic disease?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Cytokines causing macrophages to prematurely destroy RBCs

choices.label: C
choices.text: Warm autoantibodies coating the red cell membrane

choices.label: D
choices.text: Mechanical fragmentation of RBCs producing schistocytes

choices.label: E
choices.text: Splenic sequestration of RBCs from portal hypertension

explanation.summary: Inflammatory cytokines shorten the RBC lifespan by driving early macrophage-mediated destruction.
explanation.whyCorrect: Beyond reduced production, inflammation reduces RBC lifespan. Cytokines such as IL-1 and IL-6 cause macrophages to prematurely ingest and destroy RBCs, worsening the anemia.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Increased erythropoietin would raise, not lower, RBC production and is a response to anemia, not a cause.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Autoantibody-mediated (warm autoimmune) hemolysis is a distinct entity, not the mechanism taught for ACD.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Schistocyte formation from mechanical fragmentation occurs in microangiopathic hemolysis, not ACD.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Splenic sequestration from portal hypertension is a separate cause of anemia unrelated to the ACD mechanism.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with long-standing rheumatoid arthritis has a hemoglobin of 10.5 g/dL. Which of the following is the most common finding on his complete blood count indices and peripheral smear?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Normal MCV (normocytic) with a largely unremarkable smear

choices.label: C
choices.text: Markedly low MCV with prominent basophilic stippling

explanation.summary: ACD is most often normocytic with a largely normal peripheral smear.
explanation.whyCorrect: In ACD the MCV is normal or decreased, with a normal value being most common. The peripheral blood smear usually shows few abnormalities, although RBCs may be hypochromic (pale) when hemoglobin-deficient.

whyWrongByChoice.A: An elevated MCV with hypersegmented neutrophils indicates a megaloblastic anemia (B12/folate deficiency), not ACD.
whyWrongByChoice.C: A markedly low MCV with basophilic stippling suggests thalassemia or lead poisoning, not typical ACD.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Abundant schistocytes indicate microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, not ACD.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Spherocytes with a high MCHC point to hereditary spherocytosis or autoimmune hemolysis, not ACD.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with stage 4 chronic kidney disease is found to have a hemoglobin of 9.5 g/dL. Which of the following best explains the mechanism and appropriate treatment of his anemia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reduced renal erythropoietin production, treated with synthetic erythropoietin

choices.label: B
choices.text: Urinary iron loss causing iron deficiency, treated with oral iron

choices.label: C
choices.text: Hepcidin deficiency causing iron overload, treated with phlebotomy

choices.label: D
choices.text: Vitamin B12 malabsorption, treated with parenteral B12

choices.label: E
choices.text: Warm autoimmune hemolysis, treated with corticosteroids

explanation.summary: Anemia of chronic kidney disease results largely from deficient renal erythropoietin and is treated with synthetic erythropoietin.
explanation.whyCorrect: The kidney produces erythropoietin; in CKD, reduced erythropoietin leads to ineffective erythropoiesis and fewer RBCs (there is also shortened RBC lifespan from the uremic environment). This form of anemia is treated with synthetic erythropoietin injections.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Anemia of CKD is not primarily from urinary iron loss; the central problem is deficient erythropoietin.
whyWrongByChoice.C: There is no hepcidin deficiency or iron overload here; phlebotomy is not the treatment.
whyWrongByChoice.D: B12 malabsorption is unrelated to the erythropoietin-deficiency mechanism of CKD anemia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Autoimmune hemolysis is a different entity; steroids are not the treatment for anemia of CKD.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with ulcerative colitis and chronic gastrointestinal blood loss has a hemoglobin of 9.8 g/dL. Iron studies show a low serum iron AND a low ferritin. Which of the following is the best interpretation and next step?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Coexisting iron-deficiency anemia is present, and iron repletion is indicated

choices.label: B
choices.text: This is classic anemia of chronic disease, in which ferritin would be high

choices.label: D
choices.text: This is anemia of chronic kidney disease requiring erythropoietin

choices.label: E
choices.text: This is sideroblastic anemia with iron overload

explanation.summary: A low ferritin in a patient with chronic disease signals a coexisting iron deficiency, which should be treated with iron.
explanation.whyCorrect: Inflammatory GI diseases such as ulcerative colitis can cause anemia by both chronic blood loss (iron deficiency) and inflammation. Because ACD normally shows a high ferritin, a LOW ferritin here indicates a superimposed iron-deficiency anemia, so iron supplementation is warranted after ruling it in with serum studies.

whyWrongByChoice.B: In pure ACD the ferritin is normal or high; a low ferritin argues against pure ACD and points to iron deficiency.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Thalassemia trait shows microcytosis with normal or high iron studies, not a low ferritin from blood loss.
whyWrongByChoice.D: There is no indication of kidney disease; the picture is driven by GI blood loss and inflammation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Sideroblastic anemia causes iron overload with high ferritin and serum iron, the opposite of this low-ferritin picture.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old boy is evaluated for anemia. A bone marrow aspirate stained with Prussian blue reveals erythroid precursors with a ring of blue granules encircling the nucleus. The iron responsible for these granules has accumulated within which of the following structures?

explanation.summary: Ringed sideroblasts form because unchelated iron accumulates in the mitochondria of erythroblasts.
explanation.whyCorrect: In sideroblastic anemia, impaired heme synthesis leaves iron unchelated, and this iron builds up within the mitochondria. Because mitochondria are arranged around the nucleus of erythroid progenitor cells, the trapped iron creates the characteristic perinuclear ring seen as ringed sideroblasts.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The Golgi apparatus packages and modifies proteins and is not the site of iron accumulation in ringed sideroblasts.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Lysosomes degrade cellular material; the perinuclear iron ring reflects mitochondrial, not lysosomal, iron.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The rough endoplasmic reticulum is involved in protein synthesis, not in trapping the excess iron of sideroblastic anemia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The nucleolus is a site of ribosome assembly; the iron ring surrounds the nucleus within mitochondria rather than being inside the nucleolus.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with a long history of alcohol use is found to have a microcytic anemia. His physician suspects sideroblastic anemia. Which of the following is the most common finding on his peripheral blood smear?

explanation.summary: Pappenheimer bodies are the most common peripheral smear finding in sideroblastic anemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Pappenheimer bodies are iron-containing inclusions that appear as clusters of blue granules, usually at the periphery of circulating red blood cells, and reflect the abnormal iron incorporation of sideroblastic anemia. They are the most common peripheral smear finding across the various causes of the disease.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Ringed sideroblasts are nucleated erythroid progenitors confined to the bone marrow and are not seen on the peripheral blood smear.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Heinz bodies represent denatured hemoglobin and are characteristic of G6PD deficiency.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Schistocytes are fragmented red cells seen in microangiopathic hemolysis, not in sideroblastic anemia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Sickle cells are found in sickle cell disease, not in sideroblastic anemia.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man presents with fatigue over several weeks. He was recently started on isoniazid after exposure to tuberculosis. CBC shows decreased hemoglobin and decreased mean corpuscular volume. Iron studies show increased ferritin and decreased total iron-binding capacity, and the smear shows red cells with blue inclusions. Which of the following treatments would be most effective?

explanation.summary: Isoniazid-induced sideroblastic anemia results from vitamin B6 depletion and is treated with vitamin B6 supplementation.
explanation.whyCorrect: Isoniazid reduces vitamin B6 (pyridoxine) levels, and B6 is the coenzyme required for ALAS, the first enzyme of heme synthesis. Replacing vitamin B6 restores heme production and is the most effective treatment for this acquired sideroblastic anemia.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Deferoxamine is an iron chelator used for iron overload, which is uncommon in acquired sideroblastic anemia and does not correct the underlying B6 deficiency.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Phlebotomy treats iron overload but would worsen this patient's anemia and does not address the cause.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Vitamin B12 deficiency causes a macrocytic (megaloblastic) anemia, not this microcytic picture.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Folate deficiency also causes megaloblastic anemia and would not correct impaired heme synthesis.

questions.stem: A 2-month-old male is brought to the physician for failure to thrive that began shortly after birth. Workup confirms sideroblastic anemia. Which of the following is the most likely cause of his condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Dysplastic cells in the bone marrow

explanation.summary: Congenital sideroblastic anemia is most often due to an X-linked mutation that decreases ALAS activity.
explanation.whyCorrect: The patient's age and onset shortly after birth indicate a hereditary form. The most common inherited cause is an X-linked gene mutation that decreases delta-aminolevulinic acid synthase (ALAS) activity, the enzyme catalyzing the first step of heme synthesis; this predominance in males reflects X-linked inheritance.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Dysplastic marrow cells occur in myelodysplastic syndrome, an acquired disorder of adults, not a congenital cause.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Decreased ferrochelatase activity is characteristic of lead toxicity rather than hereditary sideroblastic anemia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Maternal iron deficiency during pregnancy is not a recognized cause of sideroblastic anemia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Vitamin B6 deficiency is an acquired cause from malnutrition, alcohol, or isoniazid, not a congenital disorder in a neonate.

questions.stem: A 3-year-old girl living in a house built in 1940 with peeling paint is evaluated for irritability, abdominal pain, and anemia. Her blood smear shows microcytic red cells with basophilic stippling. The suspected exposure causes sideroblastic anemia by inhibiting which of the following pairs of enzymes?

explanation.summary: Lead poisoning causes sideroblastic anemia by inhibiting ferrochelatase and ALA dehydratase.
explanation.whyCorrect: The vignette describes lead poisoning from old lead-based paint. Lead inhibits ferrochelatase (the final step inserting iron into protoporphyrin IX) and ALA dehydratase (which converts delta-ALA to porphobilinogen), impairing heme synthesis and producing sideroblastic anemia with basophilic stippling.

whyWrongByChoice.B: ALA synthase is not inhibited by lead; it is deficient in the hereditary X-linked form. Ferrochelatase alone does not describe the lead-inhibited pair.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Neither porphobilinogen deaminase nor ALA synthase is the target of lead in this disorder.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase is not the enzyme inhibited by lead in sideroblastic anemia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Although ALA dehydratase is inhibited by lead, ALA synthase is not; the correct second enzyme is ferrochelatase.

questions.stem: The first and rate-limiting step of heme synthesis converts glycine and succinyl-CoA to delta-aminolevulinic acid. The enzyme catalyzing this reaction requires which of the following as a coenzyme?

explanation.summary: ALA synthase requires vitamin B6 (pyridoxine) as a coenzyme.
explanation.whyCorrect: Delta-aminolevulinic acid synthase (ALAS) catalyzes the first, rate-limiting step of heme synthesis and requires vitamin B6 to function. This dependence explains why B6 deficiency causes sideroblastic anemia and why hereditary forms often respond to B6 supplementation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Thiamine is a coenzyme for reactions such as pyruvate dehydrogenase, not ALAS.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Riboflavin contributes to FAD/FMN-dependent reactions and is not the ALAS coenzyme.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Vitamin B12 is required for methylmalonyl-CoA and methionine metabolism, not for ALAS.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Folate participates in one-carbon transfer for nucleotide synthesis, not as the ALAS coenzyme.

questions.stem: A 56-year-old man with alcoholic cirrhosis has a low hemoglobin, low mean corpuscular volume, and rare Pappenheimer bodies on his smear. To distinguish his anemia from iron deficiency anemia, which of the following iron study patterns is most expected?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased ferritin with decreased total iron-binding capacity

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased ferritin with increased total iron-binding capacity

choices.label: C
choices.text: Decreased ferritin with decreased serum iron

choices.label: D
choices.text: Normal ferritin with increased transferrin saturation only during hemolysis

choices.label: E
choices.text: Decreased serum iron with increased total iron-binding capacity

explanation.summary: Sideroblastic anemia shows increased iron stores (high ferritin) with low TIBC, opposite to iron deficiency.
explanation.whyCorrect: Because iron cannot be incorporated into heme, iron accumulates and marrow iron stores rise, producing an increased ferritin and a decreased total iron-binding capacity. This contrasts sharply with iron deficiency anemia, in which ferritin is low and TIBC is high.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Low ferritin with high TIBC is the classic pattern of iron deficiency anemia, the opposite of sideroblastic anemia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Decreased ferritin and low serum iron indicate depleted iron stores, which is not seen in sideroblastic anemia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Sideroblastic anemia has persistently elevated iron stores rather than transient changes limited to hemolytic episodes.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Low serum iron with high TIBC again reflects iron deficiency, not the iron-replete state of sideroblastic anemia.

questions.stem: A patient with long-standing sideroblastic anemia is being monitored for secondary hemochromatosis. Which of the following findings is LEAST suggestive of the development of iron overload?

explanation.summary: Conjunctival pallor reflects the anemia itself, not the iron overload of secondary hemochromatosis.
explanation.whyCorrect: Secondary hemochromatosis results from excess iron depositing in the heart, liver, pancreas, and skin. Conjunctival pallor is a sign of low hemoglobin from the anemia and does not indicate iron overload, making it the least suggestive finding.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Cardiac iron deposition causes heart failure, which presents with bilateral peripheral pitting edema, a sign of iron overload.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hepatic iron deposition causes inflammation and elevated liver enzymes, consistent with iron overload.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Pancreatic iron injury produces diabetes mellitus with excess thirst and urination.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Iron deposition in the skin stimulates melanocytes, producing hyperpigmentation.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman has fatigue and pancytopenia. Bone marrow examination shows dysplastic erythroid precursors, and about a third of the erythroblasts are ringed sideroblasts on Prussian blue stain. Which of the following is the most likely underlying disorder?

explanation.summary: Ringed sideroblasts with marrow dysplasia in an older adult point to myelodysplastic syndrome.
explanation.whyCorrect: Myelodysplastic syndrome is a clonal hematologic neoplasm that disrupts marrow blood cell formation; approximately 30-35% of patients have ringed sideroblasts. The patient's age, pancytopenia, and dysplastic precursors make MDS the most likely acquired cause.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Iron deficiency anemia has depleted iron stores and would not produce iron-laden ringed sideroblasts.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The X-linked ALAS mutation is a hereditary disorder diagnosed in infancy and predominantly affects males.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Anemia of chronic disease results from impaired iron utilization due to inflammation but does not characteristically produce ringed sideroblasts with marrow dysplasia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Beta-thalassemia minor causes microcytosis from reduced globin chain synthesis, not ringed sideroblasts.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man who has been taking high-dose zinc supplements develops a microcytic anemia with ringed sideroblasts on bone marrow examination. Which of the following best explains the mechanism of his anemia?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Excess zinc decreases gut copper absorption, causing copper deficiency

choices.label: B
choices.text: Zinc directly inhibits ferrochelatase in erythroblasts

choices.label: C
choices.text: Zinc chelates circulating iron and prevents its delivery to the marrow

choices.label: D
choices.text: Zinc induces an X-linked mutation in the ALAS gene

choices.label: E
choices.text: Zinc accelerates hemolysis of mature erythrocytes

explanation.summary: Excess zinc lowers gut copper absorption, and the resulting copper deficiency causes sideroblastic anemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Copper deficiency is an unusual cause of sideroblastic anemia and is most often due to excess zinc taken as a supplement, because zinc decreases copper absorption in the gut. The copper deficiency then impairs normal erythropoiesis.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Ferrochelatase inhibition is the mechanism of lead poisoning, not of zinc-related copper deficiency.
whyWrongByChoice.C: The problem is copper deficiency from reduced absorption, not zinc chelation of iron.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Acquired zinc excess does not induce the hereditary X-linked ALAS mutation.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Sideroblastic anemia is an anemia of ineffective erythropoiesis, and zinc does not act by accelerating hemolysis of mature red cells here.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with hereditary sideroblastic anemia has received frequent transfusions and now has evidence of iron overload with cardiac and hepatic involvement. Which of the following is the most appropriate therapy to remove the excess iron?

explanation.summary: Iron overload in sideroblastic anemia is treated with iron chelation therapy such as deferasirox.
explanation.whyCorrect: Patients with iron overload should receive chelation therapy (for example, deferasirox) to remove excess iron. Iron overload is common in hereditary forms and is worsened by frequent transfusions, so chelation directly addresses the accumulated iron.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Additional transfusions would add more iron and worsen the overload.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Oral iron supplementation would increase the iron burden in a patient who is already iron overloaded.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Folic acid supports erythropoiesis but does not remove excess iron.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Corticosteroids are not a treatment for the iron overload of sideroblastic anemia.

questions.stem: A hematology student notes that although bone marrow examination in sideroblastic anemia reveals ringed sideroblasts, these cells are never reported on the peripheral blood smear. Which of the following best explains this observation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ringed sideroblasts are nucleated erythroid progenitors found only in the bone marrow

choices.label: B
choices.text: Ringed sideroblasts are rapidly destroyed by the spleen before entering circulation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Prussian blue stain cannot be applied to peripheral blood

choices.label: D
choices.text: Ringed sideroblasts lose their iron ring once hemoglobin synthesis is complete

choices.label: E
choices.text: Circulating red cells have too many mitochondria to display the ring

explanation.summary: Ringed sideroblasts are nucleated erythroid precursors, so they remain in the marrow and are not seen peripherally.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ringed sideroblasts are nucleated erythroid progenitor cells. Because these precursors reside in the bone marrow and do not circulate, they are present only in the marrow and are absent from the peripheral blood smear; peripheral smears instead show Pappenheimer bodies.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The reason is their location as marrow precursors, not splenic destruction of circulating ringed sideroblasts.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Prussian blue stain is in fact used on peripheral smears to demonstrate Pappenheimer bodies.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The absence is due to the cells being non-circulating precursors, not loss of the iron ring at maturity.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Mature circulating erythrocytes lack mitochondria entirely, which is why they cannot synthesize heme; this does not explain a ring being obscured.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old premenopausal woman presents with 2 months of fatigue, dizziness, and weakness. She reports 4 months of heavy menstrual bleeding with clots. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin of 10.1 g/dL (normal for females 12.0-15.5 g/dL) and a low serum ferritin, consistent with iron-deficiency anemia. Which of the following additional findings would most likely be present?

explanation.summary: Anemia is characterized by low hemoglobin, hematocrit, and RBC count, and iron deficiency produces microcytic red cells.
explanation.whyCorrect: The hemoglobin, hematocrit, and RBC count all move together and are used to diagnose anemia; a patient with anemia typically has a low hemoglobin along with a low hematocrit. Because the hematocrit tracks with hemoglobin, this patient's hematocrit would also be decreased.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Iron-deficiency anemia is a microcytic anemia, so the MCV is decreased rather than increased.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Patients with anemia have a low RBC count, not an increased one.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mean platelet volume is a platelet index and is not part of the RBC picture of iron-deficiency anemia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Most anemias are normochromic and iron deficiency is hypochromic; red cells would not be hyperchromic.

questions.stem: A newborn delivered to a 24-year-old mother who had no prenatal care is stillborn with hydrops fetalis. Genetic testing reveals deletion of all four alpha-globin genes, causing production of gamma-globin chain tetramers (hemoglobin Barts) and a diagnosis of alpha-thalassemia. Which of the following laboratory tests would most directly demonstrate this abnormal hemoglobin?

explanation.summary: Hemoglobin electrophoresis identifies the types and amounts of hemoglobin and can reveal abnormal species such as hemoglobin Barts.
explanation.whyCorrect: Hemoglobin electrophoresis separates and identifies the different hemoglobin types in the blood and is used to diagnose conditions such as thalassemia. In this form of alpha-thalassemia it would show the presence of hemoglobin Barts, the gamma-chain tetramer.

whyWrongByChoice.B: The direct antiglobulin test detects antibody or complement coating RBCs and is positive in autoimmune hemolytic anemia, not thalassemia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Osmotic fragility measures how easily RBCs lyse and is typically abnormal in hereditary spherocytosis, not alpha-thalassemia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A white blood cell differential evaluates leukocytes and would not identify an abnormal hemoglobin.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A reticulocyte count reflects marrow output but does not identify hemoglobin types.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman with worsening chronic anemia is being evaluated, and her physician wants to assess her reticulocytes. Which of the following stains is most appropriate to visualize the ribosomal RNA inside reticulocytes?

explanation.summary: Supravital stains applied to fresh blood reveal the ribosomal RNA network of reticulocytes.
explanation.whyCorrect: Supravital stains such as new methylene blue and brilliant cresyl blue are performed on fresh (living) blood and highlight structures not seen on dried smears, including the ribosomal RNA that defines reticulocytes.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Wright-Giemsa is the gold-standard differential stain for blood, but RNA is not directly visible on it (reticulocytes only appear as slightly lilac polychromatophilic cells).
whyWrongByChoice.C: Periodic acid-Schiff stains carbohydrates, not reticulocyte RNA.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Sudan black B stains lipids and is not used to visualize ribosomal RNA.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Prussian blue stains iron (for example, siderotic granules), not the RNA of reticulocytes.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman has a cold agglutinin causing red cell agglutination in her blood sample. When the complete blood count is run on the automated analyzer, which of the following parameters is most likely to remain accurate?

explanation.summary: With red cell clumping, hemoglobin stays accurate because it is measured after the red cells are lysed.
explanation.whyCorrect: When red cells clump (by agglutination or rouleaux), the analyzer counts the clumps as single large cells: the RBC count is falsely decreased and the MCV falsely increased. Hemoglobin is measured after the cells are lysed, so it usually remains accurate.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Clumped cells are counted as one, so the RBC count is falsely decreased.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Because clumps are read as single large particles, the MCV is falsely increased.
whyWrongByChoice.D: MCH is a calculated index that becomes falsely increased when the RBC count is falsely low.
whyWrongByChoice.E: MCHC is likewise falsely increased when red cell clumping distorts the RBC count and volume measurements.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with primary myelofibrosis has a peripheral blood smear examined. Which of the following red blood cell morphologies is most characteristically seen in this condition?

explanation.summary: Dacryocytes (teardrop cells) are associated with chronic myelofibrosis and other marrow-infiltrating processes.
explanation.whyCorrect: Teardrop cells (dacryocytes) are the classic red cell shape abnormality of chronic myelofibrosis and other space-occupying lesions of the marrow, where cells are deformed as they are squeezed out of a fibrotic marrow.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Target cells (codocytes) are seen in thalassemia, liver disease, and iron deficiency.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Bite cells are associated with glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Sickle cells are seen in sickle cell anemia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Schistocytes are seen in microangiopathic hemolytic anemia.

questions.stem: A 20-year-old man with a family history of hemolytic anemia has splenomegaly and jaundice, and his peripheral smear shows numerous round red cells lacking central pallor. Osmotic fragility testing is performed. Which of the following results would most likely be found?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increased osmotic fragility, with lysis at higher-than-normal NaCl concentrations

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased osmotic fragility, with lysis only in strongly hypotonic saline

choices.label: C
choices.text: Normal osmotic fragility identical to healthy controls

choices.label: D
choices.text: Complete resistance to lysis at all NaCl concentrations

choices.label: E
choices.text: Lysis only in hypertonic saline solutions

explanation.summary: Hereditary spherocytosis increases osmotic fragility because spherocytes rupture at higher-than-normal salt concentrations.
explanation.whyCorrect: The smear findings indicate hereditary spherocytosis. Spherocytes are less flexible than normal RBCs because of cytoskeleton mutations, making them prone to rupture; they are so sensitive to tonicity that they lyse even at higher-than-normal NaCl concentrations, meaning increased osmotic fragility.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Decreased osmotic fragility (resistance to lysis) is seen in sickle cell anemia and thalassemia, not hereditary spherocytosis.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Osmotic fragility is abnormal, not normal, in hereditary spherocytosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Spherocytes lyse more easily, not less; they do not resist lysis at all concentrations.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hemolysis in this test occurs in hypotonic solutions as water enters the cell, not in hypertonic solutions.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman develops fatigue and jaundice, and laboratory studies show anemia with an elevated reticulocyte count and spherocytes on the smear. Autoimmune hemolytic anemia is suspected. Which of the following tests best confirms that her red cells are coated with antibody or complement?

explanation.summary: The direct antiglobulin (Coombs) test detects antibody or complement bound to the patient's red cells.
explanation.whyCorrect: In the direct antiglobulin test, anti-immunoglobulin (Coombs) reagent is added to the patient's RBCs; if they are coated with immunoglobulin or complement, they agglutinate and the test is positive. A positive DAT is seen in autoimmune hemolytic anemia.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Hemoglobin electrophoresis identifies hemoglobin types and does not detect antibody coating of red cells.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Osmotic fragility measures how easily RBCs lyse and does not detect surface antibody or complement.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A reticulocyte count shows the marrow is responding to hemolysis but does not identify the immune cause.
whyWrongByChoice.E: RDW reflects variability in red cell size and gives no information about antibody coating.

questions.stem: During hemoglobin electrophoresis, an electrical charge is applied to a gel and the different hemoglobin types migrate away from the negatively charged electrode, traveling various distances. Which of the following correctly lists the hemoglobins in their order of travel from farthest to closest?

explanation.summary: On hemoglobin electrophoresis the order of travel from farthest to closest is A, F, S, C ("A Fat Santa Claus").
explanation.whyCorrect: As the hemoglobins migrate away from the negatively charged electrode, they land at different distances; the order from farthest to closest is hemoglobin A, then F, then S, then C, captured by the mnemonic 'A Fat Santa Claus.'

whyWrongByChoice.B: This reverses the order; C travels the least, not the most.
whyWrongByChoice.C: S and C do not lead the sequence; hemoglobin A migrates farthest.
whyWrongByChoice.D: F, not S, is the second-farthest hemoglobin, so this ordering is incorrect.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hemoglobin A migrates farthest and must come first, not F.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with anemia has a reticulocyte count that, after correction for her degree of anemia, is below 2%. Which of the following best describes what this corrected reticulocyte value indicates about her bone marrow?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The marrow is hypoproliferative and not responding appropriately

choices.label: B
choices.text: The marrow is responding briskly to ongoing hemolysis

choices.label: C
choices.text: The marrow is responding briskly to recent hemorrhage

choices.label: D
choices.text: The marrow response is normal and no anemia is present

choices.label: E
choices.text: The marrow is releasing an excess of reticulocytes into the blood

explanation.summary: A corrected reticulocyte count below 2% indicates a hypoproliferative marrow that is not responding to the anemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: The reticulocyte count should be corrected for the degree of anemia to judge whether the marrow is responding. A corrected value under 2% means the marrow is hypoproliferative and not mounting an adequate response.

whyWrongByChoice.B: A brisk response to hemolysis would raise the corrected reticulocyte count above 2%, not below.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Recent hemorrhage with a responsive marrow also drives the corrected count above 2%.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The patient is anemic, and a low corrected count reflects an inadequate marrow response, not a normal one.
whyWrongByChoice.E: An excess of reticulocytes would give a high, not low, corrected reticulocyte count.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man develops acute hemolysis after taking an oxidant medication. His peripheral smear shows red cells that appear to have had a semicircular piece removed from their edge. This bite cell morphology is most characteristic of which of the following conditions?

explanation.summary: Bite cells are the classic red cell abnormality of glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency.
explanation.whyCorrect: Bite cells are associated with glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency, in which oxidant stress damages hemoglobin and the spleen removes the denatured portion, leaving a bite-like defect.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Liver disease is classically associated with acanthocytes (spur cells), not bite cells.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hereditary spherocytosis produces spherocytes, round cells without central pallor.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Chronic myelofibrosis is associated with teardrop cells (dacryocytes).
whyWrongByChoice.E: Microangiopathic hemolytic anemia produces schistocytes, not bite cells.

questions.stem: A 3-year-old child with a history of pica and living in an old home is found to have anemia. The peripheral smear shows red cells containing coarse blue granules dispersed throughout the cytoplasm (basophilic stippling). Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause?

explanation.summary: Basophilic stippling is the red cell inclusion classically associated with lead poisoning.
explanation.whyCorrect: Basophilic stippling appears as blue granules throughout the red cell and is a recognized inclusion of lead poisoning; the clinical picture of pica and old-home exposure supports lead as the cause.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Malaria is identified by intracellular Plasmodium organisms, not basophilic stippling.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Prior splenectomy is associated with Howell-Jolly bodies, single round nuclear remnants.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Sideroblastic anemia is associated with Pappenheimer (iron-containing) bodies.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia produces spherocytes and a positive direct antiglobulin test, not basophilic stippling.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with a hemoglobinopathy has red blood cells that are resistant to lysis during automated hematology analysis. Which of the following results is most likely to occur on his complete blood count as a consequence?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Falsely low hemoglobin with a possibly elevated white blood cell count

choices.label: B
choices.text: Falsely elevated hemoglobin with a low white blood cell count

choices.label: E
choices.text: Falsely elevated red blood cell count

explanation.summary: Red cells resistant to lysis cause a falsely low hemoglobin and can be miscounted as white cells, raising the WBC count.
explanation.whyCorrect: Analyzers measure hemoglobin after lysing the red cells; when RBCs resist lysis (as in some hemoglobinopathies or in infants), less hemoglobin is released and the value is falsely low, and the intact red cells may be counted as white cells, falsely raising the WBC count.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Failure to lyse red cells lowers, rather than raises, the measured hemoglobin.
whyWrongByChoice.C: MCV is not falsely lowered by lysis-resistant cells; this scenario primarily distorts hemoglobin and the WBC count.
whyWrongByChoice.D: A falsely decreased platelet count is not the expected effect; small RBC or WBC fragments would instead falsely raise the platelet count.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Lysis resistance does not falsely elevate the RBC count; that pattern is more typical of counting artifacts unrelated to this scenario.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman is referred for purplish spots on her skin. She reports 2 weeks of easy bruising, recurrent nosebleeds, and fatigue. She takes carbamazepine for a seizure disorder. On examination she is pale with diffuse ecchymoses and petechiae. A complete blood count shows low white blood cells, low hemoglobin, and low platelets with no abnormal cells. Which of the following best describes the underlying process?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Sequestration of blood cells in an enlarged spleen

choices.label: B
choices.text: Loss of myeloid hematopoietic stem cells in the bone marrow

choices.label: C
choices.text: Infiltration of the marrow by neoplastic cells

choices.label: D
choices.text: Immune destruction of circulating mature red blood cells

choices.label: E
choices.text: Impaired hemoglobin synthesis from iron deficiency

explanation.summary: Aplastic anemia is pancytopenia caused by loss of myeloid hematopoietic stem cells.
explanation.whyCorrect: The combination of low red cells, white cells, and platelets (pancytopenia) with no abnormal cells, in a patient on carbamazepine (a recognized idiosyncratic cause), points to aplastic anemia. The ultimate mechanism regardless of etiology is loss of myeloid HSCs in the bone marrow, reducing all myeloid-derived lines.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Hypersplenism causes pancytopenia through sequestration but would present with splenomegaly, not drug-triggered marrow failure, and is not suggested here.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Marrow infiltration by leukemia or metastatic carcinoma can cause pancytopenia, but abnormal cells would typically be seen; here none are present.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Immune destruction of mature RBCs (hemolysis) causes anemia with reticulocytosis, not pancytopenia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Iron deficiency limits hemoglobin production causing microcytic anemia confined to red cells, not pancytopenia.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with newly diagnosed aplastic anemia has pancytopenia on his complete blood count. Which of the following cell types is most likely to remain within the normal range?

explanation.summary: Lymphocytes derive from lymphoid, not myeloid, progenitors and are relatively spared in aplastic anemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Aplastic anemia primarily destroys myeloid stem cells. Lymphocytes arise from the lymphoid line, so a CBC with differential shows low levels of all blood cells except lymphocytes, which remain near normal.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Platelets come from megakaryoblasts (myeloid line) and are decreased, causing bleeding.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Neutrophils come from myeloblasts (myeloid line) and are decreased, raising infection risk.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Erythrocytes come from erythroblasts (myeloid line) and are decreased, causing normocytic anemia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Monocytes come from monoblasts (myeloid line) and are decreased in aplastic anemia.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old man with suspected aplastic anemia undergoes a bone marrow biopsy. Which of the following findings would most strongly support the diagnosis?

choices.label: D
choices.text: Near-complete replacement of marrow by adipose tissue

explanation.summary: Aplastic anemia shows a hypocellular marrow in which stem cells are replaced by fat.
explanation.whyCorrect: In aplastic anemia the number of hematopoietic stem cells is markedly decreased, so the marrow becomes hypocellular and the empty spaces fill with abundant adipocytes. A biopsy confirming near-complete replacement of HSCs with adipose tissue supports the diagnosis.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Extensive fibrosis suggests primary myelofibrosis, a different cause of marrow failure that must be ruled out.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Lymphoid aggregates are not characteristic of aplastic anemia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Proliferation of erythroid precursors indicates active production, the opposite of the hypocellular marrow of aplastic anemia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Sheets of blasts indicate acute leukemia infiltrating the marrow, not aplasia.

questions.stem: A researcher studies how ionizing radiation used in radiotherapy damages the bone marrow. Which of the following best describes the mechanism by which radiation injures hematopoietic stem cells?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Generation of free radicals in a dose-dependent manner

choices.label: B
choices.text: Binding to stem cell antigens as a hapten

choices.label: C
choices.text: Direct activation of cytotoxic T cells

choices.label: E
choices.text: Sequestration of stem cells in the spleen

explanation.summary: Radiation causes dose-dependent marrow toxicity by generating free radicals.
explanation.whyCorrect: Ionizing radiation used for radiotherapy causes dose-dependent toxicity on the bone marrow by generating free radicals, which damage hematopoietic stem cells. The larger the initial and cumulative dose, the greater the risk.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Hapten binding to stem cell antigens is the mechanism of idiosyncratic drug reactions, not radiation.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Drugs and viruses lead to T-cell mediated cytotoxicity indirectly by altering antigens; radiation acts through free radicals.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Ribosomal protein dysfunction underlies Diamond-Blackfan syndrome, not radiation injury.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Splenic sequestration is a mechanism of hypersplenism, unrelated to radiation.

questions.stem: A 19-year-old woman returns from a camping trip and develops fever, myalgia, headache, and a rash on her wrists and ankles. She is diagnosed with Rocky Mountain spotted fever and treated with chloramphenicol because of a tetracycline allergy. One week later she develops worsening fatigue and mucosal bleeding, and a complete blood count shows marked decreases in red cells, white cells, and platelets. Which of the following is the best next step in management?

explanation.summary: When a causative drug is identified in aplastic anemia, the first step is to withdraw it.
explanation.whyCorrect: The patient developed pancytopenia after starting chloramphenicol, a known cause of aplastic anemia. Management of aplastic anemia is initially aimed at eliminating potential secondary causes, so the causative drug should be discontinued immediately.

whyWrongByChoice.A: N-acetylcysteine is the antidote for acetaminophen overdose, not aplastic anemia.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Merely decreasing the dose is insufficient given the life-threatening nature of aplastic anemia; the drug must be stopped.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Allogeneic transplantation carries high mortality and is reserved for severe idiopathic disease after conservative measures fail.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor is typically used for neutropenic fever after chemotherapy, not as first-line here; withdrawing the drug comes first.

questions.stem: A 5-year-old boy presents with easy bruising, and his parents are concerned about poor growth. Examination shows short stature, petechiae, and several cafe-au-lait spots, and one thumb has three phalanges. Laboratory testing reveals pancytopenia. Which of the following is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?

explanation.summary: Fanconi anemia is diagnosed by demonstrating increased chromosomal breaks with DNA cross-linking agents.
explanation.whyCorrect: The childhood onset, short stature, cafe-au-lait spots, thumb/radial abnormality (triphalangeal thumb), and pancytopenia strongly suggest Fanconi anemia. Diagnostic testing demonstrates increased chromosomal breaks in lymphocyte culture with agents that cause DNA cross-links, such as diepoxybutane (DEB) or mitomycin C.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A bone marrow biopsy would show aplasia but would not specifically establish Fanconi anemia as the cause.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hepatitis testing is useful when evaluating acquired aplastic anemia, but the constitutional features here point to Fanconi anemia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: HIV testing is part of the workup for acquired aplastic anemia, but does not confirm the suspected hereditary syndrome.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Parvovirus B19 serology is useful in pure red cell aplasia, which causes isolated anemia rather than pancytopenia.

questions.stem: A hematologist starts a patient with idiopathic aplastic anemia on eltrombopag. Which of the following best describes how this medication improves the patient's blood counts?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It suppresses cytotoxic T cells attacking stem cells

choices.label: B
choices.text: It acts as a thrombopoietin receptor agonist that increases production of all cell lines

choices.label: C
choices.text: It replaces the patient's stem cells with donor cells

choices.label: D
choices.text: It increases only the platelet count

choices.label: E
choices.text: It removes iron accumulated from transfusions

explanation.summary: Eltrombopag is a thrombopoietin receptor agonist that boosts all cell lines in aplastic anemia.
explanation.whyCorrect: Eltrombopag (EPAG) is a thrombopoietin receptor agonist acting on stem cells that increases production of all cell lines, not just platelets, making it useful in aplastic anemia.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Immunosuppression of T cells describes cyclosporine or antithymocyte globulin, not eltrombopag.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Replacing stem cells with donor cells describes allogeneic stem cell transplantation.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Although it is a thrombopoietin receptor agonist, eltrombopag stimulates all cell lines rather than platelets alone.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Chelation removes excess transfusional iron and is unrelated to eltrombopag's mechanism.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with severe idiopathic aplastic anemia has failed medical therapy and undergoes allogeneic stem cell transplantation. Which of the following is a recognized complication of this procedure?

explanation.summary: Allogeneic transplantation risks graft-versus-host disease, where donor cells attack the recipient.
explanation.whyCorrect: Allogeneic stem cell transplantation can be curative but carries a high mortality rate, so it is only considered after other treatments fail. Complications include increased infections and graft-versus-host disease, in which the transplanted cells attack the recipient.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Iron deficiency is not a characteristic complication of transplantation for aplastic anemia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: In Fanconi anemia, transplant reverses the hematologic abnormalities but not the predisposition to malignancy; it does not reverse cancer risk.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Aplastic anemia is normocytic; microcytosis points to other anemias and is not a transplant complication.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Reticulocytosis reflects active red cell production and is not a complication of transplantation.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman is being evaluated for isolated anemia. Her complete blood count shows low hemoglobin and a low reticulocyte count, but normal platelet and white blood cell counts. Bone marrow shows decreased erythroid precursors with normal granulocytes and megakaryocytes. Which of the following infections is most likely associated with this condition?

explanation.summary: Pure red cell aplasia selectively affects erythroid precursors and is associated with parvovirus B19.
explanation.whyCorrect: Pure red cell aplasia (PRCA) is anemia with decreased erythroid precursors, while granulocytes and megakaryocytes/platelets are unaffected. Acquired causes include infection with parvovirus B19, along with thymoma, chronic lymphocytic leukemia, autoimmune disorders, medications, or idiopathic cases.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Epstein-Barr virus is associated with aplastic anemia (pancytopenia), not isolated pure red cell aplasia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Hepatitis B is listed among viral causes of aplastic anemia affecting all myeloid lines.
whyWrongByChoice.D: HIV is a cause of aplastic anemia, which produces pancytopenia rather than isolated anemia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Herpes is associated with aplastic anemia, which would show pancytopenia, not selective erythroid loss.

questions.stem: A pediatrician evaluates a 6-month-old infant with pallor and fatigue during feeding. Workup reveals macrocytic anemia with reticulocytopenia, and genetic testing identifies a mutation in a gene encoding a ribosomal protein. Which of the following conditions is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Diamond-Blackfan syndrome is a congenital pure red cell aplasia caused by ribosomal protein gene mutations.
explanation.whyCorrect: Diamond-Blackfan syndrome is the most important congenital form of pure red cell aplasia. It is a rare inherited bone marrow failure syndrome associated with mutations in genes that encode ribosomal proteins, characterized by macrocytic anemia, reticulocytopenia, and cancer predisposition, usually presenting before the first birthday.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Fanconi anemia causes pancytopenia from defective DNA repair, not isolated macrocytic anemia from ribosomal protein mutations.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Idiopathic aplastic anemia produces pancytopenia and is normocytic, not an isolated congenital red cell disorder.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Iron deficiency causes microcytic anemia, not macrocytic anemia with ribosomal protein mutations.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria is an acquired clonal disorder causing hemolysis and thrombosis, not congenital pure red cell aplasia.

questions.stem: A student is asked which patient group has the worst prognosis when aplastic anemia is left untreated. Which of the following patients is at greatest risk of death within one year?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A young patient with mild disease

choices.label: B
choices.text: An elderly patient with severe disease

choices.label: C
choices.text: A young patient with moderate disease

choices.label: D
choices.text: A middle-aged patient after successful treatment

choices.label: E
choices.text: A young patient after stem cell transplantation

explanation.summary: Untreated aplastic anemia has high one-year mortality, concentrated in elderly patients with severe disease.
explanation.whyCorrect: As high as 7 out of 10 untreated patients will die within 1 year, with the majority being elderly patients and those with severe disease. Treated patients, by contrast, have a 5- to 10-year survival rate greater than 80%, especially the young with mild-to-moderate disease.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Young patients with mild disease have the best prognosis, especially when treated.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Young patients with moderate disease fare relatively well, particularly with treatment.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Successful treatment yields a survival rate above 80%, so this patient is not at greatest risk.
whyWrongByChoice.E: A young transplanted patient has been treated and does not carry the highest untreated-mortality risk.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with aplastic anemia is counseled about her expected complications. Which of the following is the least likely complication of her disease?

explanation.summary: Aplastic anemia causes cytopenias, so bleeding and infection dominate; thrombosis is not expected.
explanation.whyCorrect: Increased clot formation occurs in hypercoagulable states or malignancy. Patients with aplastic anemia have low platelets and are therefore less likely to form clots, making thrombosis the least likely complication.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Dyspnea is a manifestation of severe anemia or pulmonary infection.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Menorrhagia reflects the low platelet count causing bleeding.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Pharyngitis and other infections may increase due to neutropenia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Syncope results from low cerebral oxygen delivery seen in severe anemia.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old man of northern European descent presents with intermittent jaundice, fatigue, and left upper quadrant discomfort. His father was diagnosed with a hemolytic anemia. On examination he is icteric with splenomegaly. A peripheral smear shows round red blood cells lacking central pallor, and an eosin-5-maleimide (EMA) binding test is reduced. Which of the following defects most likely underlies this patient's condition?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Autoimmune destruction of red blood cells

choices.label: E
choices.text: Defect in the beta-chain of hemoglobin
